---
title             : "Psychometric Evaluation of the  Eysenck Personality Questionnaire-Revised Short Form (EPQ-RS-B) in Bangla"
shorttitle        : "Bangla EPQ-RS Validation"

author: 
  - name          : "Mushfiqul Anwar Siraji"
    affiliation   : "1"
    corresponding : no
    address       : "Department of History and Philosophy, North South University, Dhaka, Bangladesh"
    email         : "mushfiqul.siraji@northsouth.edu"
  - name          : "Fahria Karim"
    affiliation   : "1"
  - name          : "Shamsul Haque"
    affiliation   : "2"
    corresponding : yes
    address       : "Department of Psychology, Jeffrey Cheah School of Medicine and Health Sciences, Monash University Malaysia"
    email         : "shamsul@monash.edu"

affiliation:
  - id            : "1"
    institution   : "Department of History and Philosophy, North South University, Bangladesh"
  - id            : "2"
    institution   : "Department of Psychology, Jeffrey Cheah School of Medicine and Health Sciences, Monash University Malaysia"

authornote: |


abstract: |
  Eysenck's Psychoticism-Extraversion-Neuroticism (PEN) model remains influential in cross-cultural personality research, yet the Revised Eysenck Personality Questionnaire Short Form (EPQ-RS) has never been adapted for Bangla-speaking populations. We developed and evaluated a Bangla version (EPQ-RS-B) in a community sample of 550 Bangladeshi adults (320 women, 230 men; *M*~age~ = 29.02, *SD* = 7.63). Psychometric properties were assessed through multiple complementary approaches: internal consistency (Cronbach's $\alpha$, ordinal $\alpha$, McDonald's $\omega_t$), exploratory structural equation modeling (ESEM) with target rotation, confirmatory factor analysis (CFA), two-parameter logistic (2PL) item response theory (IRT) models, sex-based measurement invariance, and concurrent validity with the Bangla Big Five Inventory-2 (BFI-2-B). Results indicated that Extraversion, Neuroticism, and the Lie scale demonstrated acceptable reliability and structural validity, consistent with cross-cultural patterns. The Psychoticism scale, however, exhibited low internal consistency, weak item discrimination, and poor measurement precision, replicating the well-documented fragility of this subscale across languages and cultures. ESEM yielded a cleaner factor structure than strict CFA. Concurrent validity analyses confirmed theoretically expected convergent and discriminant associations between EPQ-RS-B subscales and BFI-2 domains. These findings support the use of the EPQ-RS-B Extraversion and Neuroticism scales in Bangladeshi research contexts but urge caution with the Psychoticism subscale. We discuss implications for cross-cultural PEN assessment and the comparative utility of the Giant Three and Big Five frameworks.

keywords          : "EPQ-RS, Bangla adaptation, PEN model, psychometric validation, IRT, ESEM, Big Five, concurrent validity"
wordcount         : "X"

bibliography      : "references.bib"

floatsintext      : no
linenumbers       : yes
draft             : no
mask              : no

documentclass     : "apa7"
classoption       : "man"
output            : papaja::apa6_docx
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo = FALSE,
  message = FALSE,
  warning = FALSE,
  fig.width = 7,
  fig.height = 5,
  cache = TRUE
)

set.seed(123)

# Output folders
for (p in c(
  "outputs", "outputs/tables", "outputs/figures",
  "outputs/models", "outputs/irt","outputs/ESEM",
  "outputs/irt/bfi_facets", "outputs/irt/epq_scales"
)) dir.create(p, recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)
```

```{r packages, include=FALSE}
suppressPackageStartupMessages({
  library(papaja)
  library(tidyverse)
  library(psych)
  library(lavaan)
  library(semTools)
  library(mirt)
  library(gtsummary)
  library(janitor)
  library(broom)
  library(writexl)
  library(mirt)
library(ggplot2)
library(cowplot)
library(dplyr)
library(ggsci)
  library(kutils)
library(gt)
library(gtExtras)

})

# Load esemComp if available
has_esemcomp <- requireNamespace("esemComp", quietly = TRUE)
if (has_esemcomp) library(esemComp)
```


# Introduction

## Eysenck's PEN Model and Its Measurement Legacy

Few personality taxonomies have generated as sustained a programme of cross-cultural research as H. J. Eysenck's Psychoticism-Extraversion-Neuroticism (PEN) model. Rooted in a biologically oriented theory of individual differences, the model posits three broad, largely independent dimensions of personality: Extraversion (E), reflecting sociability, activity, and positive affect; Neuroticism (N), reflecting emotional instability, anxiety, and proneness to negative affect; and Psychoticism (P), a dimension originally conceived as a continuum anchoring normal personality variation to psychotic and antisocial tendencies [@eysenck1985revised]. Across more than three decades, these constructs have been measured by a succession of questionnaires, from the Maudsley Personality Inventory through the Eysenck Personality Inventory, the Eysenck Personality Questionnaire (EPQ), and its subsequent revision, the EPQ-R [@ruch2021long].

The Revised Eysenck Personality Questionnaire Short Form (EPQ-RS) was introduced by @eysenck1985revised as a 48-item self-report inventory containing 12 items for each of four subscales: Psychoticism, Extraversion, Neuroticism, and a Lie/Social Desirability (L) scale. This short form was developed primarily to improve the psychometric properties of the Psychoticism scale, which in the original EPQ had suffered from severely skewed distributions, low reliability, and inconsistent factor loadings [@eysenck1985revised]. In the original English-speaking validation samples, @eysenck1985revised reported adequate internal consistency for E (males .88, females .84), N (males .84, females .80), and L (males .77, females .73), but P remained conspicuously weaker (males .62, females .61). Six original EPQ P items were dropped entirely for lacking consistent loadings on the Psychoticism factor.

## Cross-Cultural Psychometric Evidence: A Pattern of Persistent P-Scale Weakness


Cross-cultural psychometric evaluations of the EPQ-RS have revealed a consistent and troubling pattern: whereas Extraversion and Neuroticism typically achieve acceptable internal consistency (alphas generally above .70), the Psychoticism scale performs poorly across virtually every language adaptation examined to date (see Supplementary Table 1 for a detailed summary). Francis et al. (1992) first documented this discrepancy across four English-speaking samples, and subsequent adaptations in Japanese (Hosokawa & Ohyama, 1993), Hebrew (Katz & Francis, 2000), German (Francis et al., 2006), Greek (Alexopoulos, 2004; Morfaki et al., 2021), Hindi (Tiwari & Singh, 2009), and Spanish (Aluja et al., 2003) have consistently replicated P alphas ranging from as low as .24 to rarely exceeding .55. Item-level analyses across several of these studies have identified individual P items with negligible or near-zero corrected item-total correlations, suggesting that the scale's poor reliability reflects substantive problems with item content rather than mere sampling variability.

Beyond internal consistency, confirmatory factor analyses have raised additional structural concerns. CFA fit to the hypothesized four-factor simple structure has generally been unsatisfactory, with modification indices revealing systematic local dependencies among items, particularly within the N, E, and P scales (Aluja et al., 2003). In the Greek adaptation, multiple items produced non-significant factor loadings (Alexopoulos, 2004), a finding independently corroborated by Morfaki et al. (2021). It is worth noting, however, that several of these validation studies relied on restricted samples (e.g., single-sex undergraduate cohorts, modest sample sizes) or omitted factor analysis entirely, limiting the strength of conclusions that can be drawn about dimensional structure. Nevertheless, the convergence of evidence across languages, samples, and analytic approaches suggests that the psychometric limitations of the EPQ-RS, and of the P scale in particular, are not artifacts of any single translation but reflect deeper structural weaknesses in the instrument itself.

More recent work has extended evaluation beyond classical test theory. Colledani et al. (2018) applied two-parameter logistic item response theory models to develop an improved Italian short form, selecting items on the basis of discrimination, difficulty, and differential item functioning. The resulting instrument outperformed the original EPQ-RS on most psychometric criteria, though P remained the weakest scale even after optimization. Pflanz et al. (2024), working with UK Biobank data from over 400,000 respondents, demonstrated that the 12-item N scale lacked information coverage at the score distribution extremes and that a reduced 7-item version offered greater measurement efficiency. These IRT-based findings complement the classical reliability evidence by showing that item-level measurement precision is unevenly distributed across the trait continuum, a limitation that alpha coefficients alone cannot detect.

At the level of cross-cultural measurement invariance, the picture is equally sobering. McLarnon et al. (2020) applied the alignment method to EPQ-RS data from over 43,000 respondents across 35 countries and found that Neuroticism items exhibited the highest rates of non-invariance, suggesting that raw observed N scores have weak cross-country comparability without statistical correction. This finding calls into question the practice of interpreting mean-level differences in N across populations at face value. Eysenck and Barrett (2013) further clarified that the Kaiser-Hunka-Bianchini "similarities" reported in earlier cross-national EPQ studies were transformation cosines rather than true congruence coefficients, a methodological correction that tempers some of the stronger invariance claims made in the earlier PEN literature (cf. Barrett et al., 1998).

In a comprehensive retrospective analysis spanning 50 years of Eysenck personality instruments, Ruch et al. (2021) provided additional evidence that P's psychometric difficulties are partly a consequence of construct drift. Their item-difficulty analysis revealed that P items had been progressively "softened" across successive revisions, shifting content from primarily low Agreeableness toward a mixture that also incorporates low Conscientiousness. This heterogeneity is not merely a measurement nuisance; it has direct theoretical implications, because a scale whose content shifts across revisions cannot be said to operationalize a stable construct. Ruch et al. judged the EPQ-R to be the most valid single PEN measure among Eysenck's instruments, but acknowledged the inherent incoherence of the P domain.

In sum, the cross-cultural literature reveals a reliable pattern: E and N are psychometrically robust across languages and sample compositions; L is generally adequate though occasionally weak; and P consistently fails to meet conventional standards for internal consistency, factor coherence, or measurement precision. This pattern is not merely a nuisance of short-form measurement; it reflects deeper conceptual ambiguities in the Psychoticism construct itself. These accumulated weaknesses raise a natural question: if the Giant Three framework is to be retained in applied personality research, how does it perform when evaluated alongside its principal contemporary competitor, the Big Five? Despite the extensive literature on each framework separately, direct within-sample comparisons using modern psychometric methods remain surprisingly scarce, particularly in South Asian populations where neither framework has received thorough evaluation. The present study addresses this gap by comparing the structural validity, item-level measurement precision, and criterion-related predictive utility of the Bangla adaptations of the BFI-2 and EPQ-RS in a Bangladeshi adult sample.

## The PEN-Big Five Interface and Concurrent Validity

A longstanding question in personality assessment concerns how Eysenck's three-factor PEN model maps onto the Five-Factor Model (FFM; also known as the Big Five). The theoretical and empirical relationships between the two frameworks are well established but asymmetric. Extraversion in both models captures overlapping variance related to sociability, assertiveness, and positive emotionality. Neuroticism in the PEN model corresponds closely to the Big Five domain of Negative Emotionality (or Neuroticism). The more contentious alignment involves Psychoticism, which has been shown to reflect a blend of low Agreeableness and low Conscientiousness in FFM terms, rather than constituting a unitary dimension orthogonal to both [@mccrae1985comparison; @draycott1995kline; @saggino2000big; @scholte2004bruyn; @heaven2013big; @ruch2021long].

The Big Five Inventory-2 [BFI-2; @soto2017bfi2] provides a hierarchical measurement architecture with five domains, each decomposed into three facets (15 facets total), offering greater bandwidth and fidelity than the original BFI. A validated Bangla adaptation (BFI-2-B) has recently been published [@siraji2025bangla], reporting satisfactory domain-level reliability ($\alpha$ = .76 to .86; $\omega_t$ = .77 to .86) and adequate facet-level reliability for most facets.

Concurrent validity between PEN and Big Five instruments is typically evaluated through zero-order correlations. The expected pattern, supported by substantial prior work, includes strong positive correlations between EPQ-RS E and BFI-2 Extraversion, strong positive correlations between EPQ-RS N and BFI-2 Negative Emotionality, and moderate negative correlations between EPQ-RS P and both BFI-2 Agreeableness and BFI-2 Conscientiousness [@mccrae1985comparison; @colledani2018irt]. EPQ-RS L has been linked to impression management and social desirability, with modest positive associations with Agreeableness and Conscientiousness reported in some studies.

## The Present Study

Despite the extensive cross-cultural programme surrounding the EPQ-RS, no Bangla-language adaptation exists. Bangladesh, with a population exceeding 170 million, represents a substantial gap in the PEN measurement literature. Moreover, the overwhelming majority of EPQ-RS validation studies have relied on classical test theory alone, with only a handful incorporating IRT or modern structural equation modeling approaches such as ESEM.

The present study addresses this gap by developing and evaluating a Bangla adaptation of the EPQ-RS (EPQ-RS-B) using a comprehensive, multi-method psychometric strategy. Specifically, we examine: (a) internal consistency reliability using multiple estimators (Cronbach's $\alpha$, ordinal $\alpha$ from tetrachoric correlations, and McDonald's $\omega_t$); (b) structural validity through both ESEM with target rotation and CFA, as applied to the dichotomous item structure; (c) item-level measurement quality through 2PL IRT models; (d) sex-based measurement invariance; and (e) concurrent validity with the previously validated BFI-2-B [@siraji2025bangla]. Given the cross-cultural evidence reviewed above, we expected that E and N would demonstrate acceptable psychometric properties, that P would exhibit the characteristic weaknesses observed in virtually every prior adaptation, and that the pattern of concurrent validity with BFI-2 domains would conform to established PEN-FFM correspondence.

# Method

## Participants

```{r data-import, include=FALSE}
raw_data <- readRDS("raw_data.rds")

```

```{r helper-functions, include=FALSE}
make_squished_map <- function(x) {
  setNames(unname(x), stringr::str_squish(names(x)))
}

recode_from_map <- function(x, map_vec) {
  if (is.numeric(x)) return(as.numeric(x))
  x_chr <- stringr::str_squish(as.character(x))
  as.numeric(unname(map_vec[x_chr]))
}

row_mean_safe <- function(df, cols) {
  rowMeans(df[, cols, drop = FALSE], na.rm = TRUE) |> as.numeric()
}

row_sum_safe <- function(df, cols) {
  rowSums(df[, cols, drop = FALSE], na.rm = TRUE) |> as.numeric()
}

safe_cor_matrix <- function(dat, item_type = c("ordinal", "binary")) {
  item_type <- match.arg(item_type)
  out <- tryCatch(
    {
      if (item_type == "binary") psych::tetrachoric(dat)$rho else psych::polychoric(dat)$rho
    },
    error = function(e) NULL
  )
  if (!is.null(out)) {
    out <- tryCatch(psych::cor.smooth(out), error = function(e) out)
  }
  out
}

safe_alpha <- function(dat) {
  tryCatch(psych::alpha(dat)$total$raw_alpha, error = function(e) NA_real_)
}

safe_ordinal_alpha <- function(dat, item_type = c("ordinal", "binary")) {
  item_type <- match.arg(item_type)
  rho <- safe_cor_matrix(dat, item_type = item_type)
  if (is.null(rho)) return(NA_real_)
  tryCatch(psych::alpha(rho, n.obs = nrow(dat))$total$raw_alpha, error = function(e) NA_real_)
}

safe_omega_total <- function(dat, item_type = c("ordinal", "binary")) {
  item_type <- match.arg(item_type)
  rho <- safe_cor_matrix(dat, item_type = item_type)
  if (is.null(rho)) return(NA_real_)
  tryCatch(psych::omega(rho, nfactors = 1, plot = FALSE)$omega.tot, error = function(e) NA_real_)
}

extract_mirt_pars <- function(mod, scale_name) {
  cf <- coef(mod, IRTpars = TRUE, simplify = TRUE)$items |>
    as.data.frame() |>
    tibble::rownames_to_column("Item") |>
    mutate(Scale = scale_name, .before = 1)
  cf
}

make_tif_summary <- function(mod, scale_name, theta = seq(-4, 4, .1)) {
  Theta <- matrix(theta)
  tif <- testinfo(mod, Theta = Theta)
  tibble(
    Scale = scale_name,
    peak_info = max(tif, na.rm = TRUE),
    theta_at_peak = theta[which.max(tif)],
    mean_info_m2_to_2 = mean(tif[theta >= -2 & theta <= 2], na.rm = TRUE)
  )
}

cv_r2_full <- function(formula, data, k = 10, seed = 123) {
  set.seed(seed)
  d <- model.frame(formula, data = data) |> tidyr::drop_na()
  n <- nrow(d)
  folds <- sample(rep(1:k, length.out = n))
  y_name <- all.vars(formula)[1]

  fold_r2 <- numeric(k)
  for (i in seq_len(k)) {
    train <- d[folds != i, , drop = FALSE]
    test  <- d[folds == i, , drop = FALSE]
    fit   <- lm(formula, data = train)
    pred  <- predict(fit, newdata = test)
    y     <- test[[y_name]]
    fold_r2[i] <- 1 - sum((y - pred)^2) / sum((y - mean(y))^2)
  }

  m <- mean(fold_r2, na.rm = TRUE)
  s <- sd(fold_r2, na.rm = TRUE)
  se <- s / sqrt(k)
  tcrit <- qt(.975, df = k - 1)

  tibble(
    mean_r2 = m, sd_r2 = s,
    ci_lower = m - tcrit * se, ci_upper = m + tcrit * se,
    negative_folds = sum(fold_r2 < 0), n = n, k = k
  )
}
```

```{r demographics-prep, include=FALSE}
demographics <- raw_data |>
  transmute(
    age = as.numeric(Age),
    gender = dplyr::case_when(
      Gender == "পুরুষ" ~ "Male",
      Gender == "স্ত্রী" ~ "Female",
      TRUE ~ as.character(Gender)
    ) |> factor(),
    religion = factor(Religion) |>
      forcats::fct_recode(
        Islam = "ইসলাম",
        Hindu = "সনাতন হিন্দু",
        Buddhism = "বৌদ্ধ",
        Christianity = "খ্রিস্টান",
        Others = "অন্যান্য"
      ),
    marital_status = factor(Marital_Status) |>
      forcats::fct_recode(
        Married = "বিবাহিত",
        Unmarried = "অবিবাহিত",
        Divorced = "ডিভোর্সড"
      ),
    occupation = dplyr::case_when(
      Occupation == "সরকারি চাকরিজীবী" ~ "Service Holder",
      Occupation == "ছাত্র" ~ "Student",
      TRUE ~ as.character(Occupation)
    ) |> factor(),
    education = factor(Education_Years) |>
      forcats::fct_recode(
        Undergraduate = "স্নাতক",
        Postgraduate = "স্নাতকোত্তর",
        PhD = "ডক্টর অব ফিলোসফি"
      ),
    ses = as.numeric(SES)
  )

sample_n <- nrow(demographics)
sample_n
```

The sample comprised `r sample_n` Bangladeshi adults recruited from Dhaka (320 women, 230 men; *M*~age~ = 29.02, *SD* = 7.63; range 18 to 60). The majority identified as Muslim (85%), held service-sector employment (62%) or were students (38%), and reported undergraduate (52%) or postgraduate (48%) educational attainment. Mean socioeconomic status was 6.47 (*SD* = 1.91) on a 10-point self-report scale. All participants provided written informed consent; the study was approved by the Institutional Review Board of North South University, Bangladesh.

## Materials

### Big Five Inventory-2 (BFI-2-B)

The Big Five Inventory-2 [BFI-2; @soto2017bfi2] is a 60-item self-report measure designed to capture five personality domains (Extraversion, Agreeableness, Conscientiousness, Negative Emotionality, and Open-Mindedness) and 15 facet traits (three per domain; four items per facet). Items are rated on a 5-point Likert-type agreement scale (1 = *Disagree strongly* to 5 = *Agree strongly*). The present study used the Bangla adaptation (BFI-2-B), which has been psychometrically validated for use in Bangladesh [@siraji2025bangla]. @siraji2025bangla reported satisfactory domain-level reliability ($\alpha$ = .76 to .86; $\omega_t$ = .77 to .86) in a community sample of 646 participants.

### Revised Eysenck Personality Questionnaire Short Form (EPQ-RS-B)

The Giant Three personality dimensions were assessed using the EPQ-RS [@eysenck1985revised], a 48-item self-report inventory measuring Psychoticism (P), Extraversion (E), Neuroticism (N), and a Lie/Social Desirability scale (L), with 12 items per subscale. Items use a dichotomous (Yes/No) response format. For the present study, we developed a Bangla adaptation (EPQ-RS-B) following the International Test Commission's guidelines for test translation and adaptation [@bartram2018itc]. The adaptation employed a committee-based forward-backward translation procedure with two independent forward translations and two independent back-translations; each stage was reconciled through team synthesis, and the final version was compared against the original to resolve semantic and conceptual discrepancies. Content validity was evaluated by 15 subject-matter experts using item-level and scale-level content validity indices (I-CVI and S-CVI), which indicated satisfactory content validity [@polit2007cvi].

## Procedure

Data collection took place in Dhaka. The questionnaire battery was administered in Bangla in a fixed order: BFI-2-B, EPQ-RS-B, and demographic questionnaire. This order was held constant across all participants; potential order effects are acknowledged as a limitation. The full battery required approximately 35 to 45 minutes. No incentives were provided.

All data were screened for incomplete responses. Participants who failed to complete more than 10% of any single instrument were excluded. The final analytic sample of `r sample_n` participants comprised complete data across all measures.

## Analysis Plan

All analyses were conducted in R.  In the IRT analyses, marginal reliability and empirical reliability were additionally reported from the fitted models.
### Descriptive analyses and Reliability
Descriptive statistics were computed using the *psych* package, and reliability estimates were obtained using *psych* and *semTools*. For the dichotomous EPQ-RS-B items, we reported item endorsement proportions and descriptive indices at the item level, and we summarized internal consistency at the subscale level using Cronbach’s alpha, and McDonald’s omega. 

### Structural validity
The latent structure of the Bangla EPQ-RS-B was examined using exploratory structural equation modeling (ESEM) via *esemComp*. Competing three-factor and four-factor solutions were estimated, with all items treated as ordered categorical indicators and modeled using weighted least squares with mean and variance adjustment (WLSMV) under theta parameterization. An oblique target rotation was used so that hypothesized primary loadings were estimated freely while allowing non-target loadings to remain near, but not fixed at, zero. Model adequacy was summarized using the scaled chi-square statistic, the comparative fit index (CFI), the Tucker-Lewis index (TLI), the root mean square error of approximation (RMSEA), and the standardized root mean square residual (SRMR), and the preferred solution was selected on the basis of overall fit, interpretability, and the substantive clarity of the loading pattern (Asparouhov & Muthén, 2009). [Add hu & Bentler's Cutoff]

### Measurement invariance across sex and language
Measurement invariance was evaluated across sex (within the current sample) and across  English (UK)  versions (on the 44 common items between EPQ-RS and EPQ data set from XX) using multigroup categorical factor models fit . We tested progressively more restrictive models beginning with configural invariance and then imposing equality constraints on factor loadings, thresholds, and more stringent structural parameters as appropriate. Invariance was retained when the decrease in CFI did not exceed .010 and the increase in RMSEA did not exceed .015, while also considering the overall pattern of results across nested models (Meredith, 1993; Vandenberg & Lance, 2000; Cheung & Rensvold, 2002; Chen, 2007). 

###EPQ-RS alignment benchmarking analysis

To evaluate the cross-cultural comparability of Bangla EPQ-RS-B scores, we benchmarked the Bangla sample against the archival cross-cultural EPQ files and restricted the analysis to the 44 binary items shared across the Bangla and archival datasets. The UK-scored archival files were used as the benchmark set, and England served as the reference group. Following the logic of the factor alignment method, we fit separate one-factor configural models for Psychoticism, Extraversion, Neuroticism, and Lie using lavaan, treating all indicators as ordered categorical and estimating the models with WLSMV under theta parameterization. Unstandardized factor loadings and thresholds were extracted and passed to the sirt alignment routine to recover group-specific latent means and variances under approximate invariance. The aligned factor means and standard deviations were then re-expressed relative to England (M = 0, SD = 1). We examined aligned latent means and variances, residual non-invariance in loadings and thresholds, and alignment R² values to judge the extent to which Bangla scores could be placed on a common latent metric with the archival benchmark countries (Asparouhov & Muthén, 2014; Eysenck & Barrett, 2013; McLarnon & Romero, 2020). 

### IRT analyses
IRT analyses were conducted separately for the Psychoticism, Extraversion, Neuroticism, and Lie subscales using unidimensional two-parameter logistic (2PL) models estimated in the mirt package with maximum marginal likelihood and the Metropolis-Hastings Robbins-Monro (MHRM) algorithm. For each subscale, we reported item discrimination (a) and difficulty/location (b) parameters, the M2 limited-information model-fit statistic, marginal reliability, empirical reliability, item fit, and person fit. Item fit was evaluated using the S-X² statistic, together with infit and outfit mean-square indices. Person fit was examined using Zh, with values below -2.00 flagged as aberrant response patterns. We also inspected item characteristic curves (ICCs), item information curves (IICs), and test information curves (TICs) across the latent continuum to evaluate monotonicity, item-level precision, and scale-level precision. These diagnostics were integrated to classify items as satisfactory, borderline, or problematic within each subscale (Cai, 2010; Cai et al., 2006; Chalmers, 2012; Drasgow et al., 1985; Orlando & Thissen, 2003). 

### Sex-based differential item functioning
Sex-based differential item functioning (DIF) was examined separately for the Psychoticism, Extraversion, Neuroticism, and Lie subscales using multiple-group 2PL IRT models. Because the EPQ-RS-B items were dichotomously scored, analyses were conducted within each subscale after retaining only participants with complete item and sex data and items showing valid binary responses with nonzero variance in both sex groups. Female participants were treated as the reference group. For each subscale, an anchored baseline model was first estimated in which all item parameters were constrained to equality across groups, while the latent mean and variance were freely estimated in the focal group. Item-level likelihood-ratio tests were then used to evaluate uniform DIF by freeing the item location parameter, nonuniform DIF by freeing the discrimination parameter, and combined DIF by freeing both simultaneously. The combined DIF test was treated as the primary screening criterion because it captured both forms of item noninvariance within a single comparison. Because multiple item-level tests were conducted within each subscale, p values were adjusted using the Benjamini-Hochberg false discovery rate procedure. Items with adjusted p values below .05 in the combined DIF analysis were flagged as showing potential sex-based DIF. Partially anchored multiple-group 2PL models were then estimated using non-flagged items as anchors and allowing flagged items to vary across groups, and these models were used to obtain group-specific a and b parameters for interpretation (Benjamini & Hochberg, 1995; Chalmers, 2012; Kim & Cohen, 1995). 
### Concurrent validity
Concurrent validity was examined using bivariate correlations between the EPQ-RS-B subscales  BFI-2-B domains/facets  

References
Asparouhov, T., & Muthén, B. (2009). Exploratory structural equation modeling. Structural Equation Modeling: A Multidisciplinary Journal, 16(3), 397–438. https://doi.org/10.1080/10705510903008204
Asparouhov, T., & Muthén, B. (2014). Multiple-group factor analysis alignment. Structural Equation Modeling: A Multidisciplinary Journal, 21(4), 495–508. https://doi.org/10.1080/10705511.2014.919210
Benjamini, Y., & Hochberg, Y. (1995). Controlling the false discovery rate: A practical and powerful approach to multiple testing. Journal of the Royal Statistical Society: Series B (Methodological), 57(1), 289–300. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.2517-6161.1995.tb02031.x
Cai, L. (2010). High-dimensional exploratory item factor analysis by a Metropolis-Hastings Robbins-Monro algorithm. Psychometrika, 75(1), 33–57. https://doi.org/10.1007/s11336-009-9136-x
Cai, L., Maydeu-Olivares, A., Coffman, D. L., & Thissen, D. (2006). Limited-information goodness-of-fit testing of item response theory models for sparse 2^P tables. British Journal of Mathematical and Statistical Psychology, 59(1), 173–194. https://doi.org/10.1348/000711005X66419
Chalmers, R. P. (2012). mirt: A multidimensional item response theory package for the R environment. Journal of Statistical Software, 48(6), 1–29. https://doi.org/10.18637/jss.v048.i06
Chen, F. F. (2007). Sensitivity of goodness of fit indexes to lack of measurement invariance. Structural Equation Modeling: A Multidisciplinary Journal, 14(3), 464–504. https://doi.org/10.1080/10705510701301834
Cheung, G. W., & Rensvold, R. B. (2002). Evaluating goodness-of-fit indexes for testing measurement invariance. Structural Equation Modeling: A Multidisciplinary Journal, 9(2), 233–255. https://doi.org/10.1207/S15328007SEM0902_5
Drasgow, F., Levine, M. V., & Williams, E. A. (1985). Appropriateness measurement with polychotomous item response models and standardized indices. British Journal of Mathematical and Statistical Psychology, 38(1), 67–86. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.2044-8317.1985.tb00817.x
Eysenck, S., & Barrett, P. (2013). Re-introduction to cross-cultural studies of the EPQ. Personality and Individual Differences, 54(4), 485–489. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.paid.2012.09.022
Kim, S.-H., & Cohen, A. S. (1995). A comparison of Lord’s chi-square, Raju’s area measures, and the likelihood ratio test on detection of differential item functioning. Applied Measurement in Education, 8(4), 291–312. https://doi.org/10.1207/S15324818AME0804_2
McCrae, R. R., & Costa, P. T., Jr. (1985). Comparison of EPI and psychoticism scales with measures of the five-factor model of personality. Personality and Individual Differences, 6(5), 587–597. https://doi.org/10.1016/0191-8869(85)90008-X
McLarnon, M. J. W., & Romero, E. F. (2020). Cross-cultural equivalence of shortened versions of the Eysenck Personality Questionnaire: An application of the alignment method. Personality and Individual Differences, 163, Article 110074. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.paid.2020.110074
Meredith, W. (1993). Measurement invariance, factor analysis and factorial invariance. Psychometrika, 58(4), 525–543. https://doi.org/10.1007/BF02294825
Mor, N. (2010). Eysenck personality questionnaire. In I. B. Weiner & W. E. Craighead (Eds.), The Corsini encyclopedia of psychology (pp. 1–2). Wiley.
Orlando, M., & Thissen, D. (2003). Further investigation of the performance of S-X²: An item fit index for use with dichotomous item response theory models. Applied Psychological Measurement, 27(4), 289–298. https://doi.org/10.1177/0146621603027004004
Vandenberg, R. J., & Lance, C. E. (2000). A review and synthesis of the measurement invariance literature: Suggestions, practices, and recommendations for organizational research. Organizational Research Methods, 3(1), 4–70. https://doi.org/10.1177/109442810031002
If you want, I can next turn this into a single continuous Method subsection with no headings, ready to paste into the manuscript.


# Results

```{r bfi-scoring, include=FALSE}
bfi_map <- make_squished_map(c(
  "একেবারেই সমর্থন করি না" = 1,
  "সমর্থন করি না" = 2,
  "অনিশ্চিত" = 3,
  "সমর্থন করি" = 4,
  "খুব বেশি  সমর্থন করি" = 5,
  "খুব বেশি সমর্থন করি" = 5
))
bfi_raw <- raw_data |> select(BF_1:BF_60)
names(bfi_raw) <- paste0("BFI", sprintf("%02d", 1:60))
bfi_items <- bfi_raw |> mutate(across(everything(), ~ recode_from_map(.x, bfi_map)))

bfi_reverse <- c(3,4,5,8,9,11,12,16,17,22,23,24,25,26,28,29,30,31,
                 36,37,42,44,45,47,48,49,50,51,55,58)
for (i in bfi_reverse) {
  nm  <- sprintf("BFI%02d", i)
  rnm <- sprintf("RBFI%02d", i)
  bfi_items[[rnm]] <- 6 - bfi_items[[nm]]
}

bfi_facet_items <- list(
  Sociability            = c("BFI01","RBFI16","RBFI31","BFI46"),
  Assertiveness          = c("BFI06","BFI21","RBFI36","RBFI51"),
  Energy_Level           = c("RBFI11","RBFI26","BFI41","BFI56"),
  Compassion             = c("BFI02","RBFI17","BFI32","RBFI47"),
  Respectfulness         = c("BFI07","RBFI22","RBFI37","BFI52"),
  Trust                  = c("RBFI12","BFI27","RBFI42","BFI57"),
  Organization           = c("RBFI03","BFI18","BFI33","RBFI48"),
  Productiveness         = c("RBFI08","RBFI23","BFI38","BFI53"),
  Responsibility         = c("BFI13","RBFI28","BFI43","RBFI58"),
  Anxiety                = c("RBFI04","BFI19","BFI34","RBFI49"),
  Depression             = c("RBFI09","RBFI24","BFI39","BFI54"),
  Emotional_Volatility   = c("BFI14","RBFI29","RBFI44","BFI59"),
  Intellectual_Curiosity = c("BFI10","RBFI25","BFI40","RBFI55"),
  Aesthetic_Sensitivity  = c("RBFI05","BFI20","BFI35","RBFI50"),
  Creative_Imagination   = c("BFI15","RBFI30","RBFI45","BFI60")
)

bfi_domain_items <- list(
  Extraversion = c("BFI01","BFI06","RBFI11","RBFI16","BFI21","RBFI26","RBFI31","RBFI36","BFI41","BFI46","RBFI51","BFI56"),
  Agreeableness = c("BFI02","BFI07","RBFI12","RBFI17","RBFI22","BFI27","BFI32","RBFI37","RBFI42","RBFI47","BFI52","BFI57"),
  Conscientiousness = c("RBFI03","RBFI08","BFI13","BFI18","RBFI23","RBFI28","BFI33","BFI38","BFI43","RBFI48","BFI53","RBFI58"),
  Negative_Emotionality = c("RBFI04","RBFI09","BFI14","BFI19","RBFI24","RBFI29","BFI34","BFI39","RBFI44","RBFI49","BFI54","BFI59"),
  Open_Mindedness = c("RBFI05","BFI10","BFI15","BFI20","RBFI25","RBFI30","BFI35","BFI40","RBFI45","RBFI50","RBFI55","BFI60")
)

bfi_facets <- purrr::imap_dfc(bfi_facet_items, ~ tibble(!!.y := row_mean_safe(bfi_items, .x)))
bfi_domains <- purrr::imap_dfc(bfi_domain_items, ~ tibble(!!.y := row_mean_safe(bfi_items, .x)))
```

```{r epq-scoring, include=FALSE}
epq_items <-  raw_data |>
  select(EYP1:EYP48) |>
  mutate(across(everything(), ~ case_when(
    as.character(.x) == "হ্যাঁ" ~ 1,
    as.character(.x) == "না" ~ 0,
    TRUE ~ suppressWarnings(as.numeric(as.character(.x)))
  )))

epq_reverse <- c(
 "EYP2" , "EYP6","EYP8","EYP12","EYP18","EYP20","EYP24","EYP26","EYP27","EYP28",
  "EYP29","EYP33","EYP35","EYP37","EYP40","EYP41","EYP43","EYP47"
)

epq_items <- epq_items |>
  mutate(across(all_of(epq_reverse), ~ ifelse(is.na(.x), NA_real_, 1 - .x)))

epq_item_sets <- list(
  Psychoticism = c("EYP10","EYP14","EYP22","EYP31","EYP39","EYP2","EYP6","EYP18","EYP26","EYP28","EYP35","EYP43"),
  Extraversion = c("EYP3","EYP7","EYP11","EYP15","EYP19","EYP23","EYP32","EYP36","EYP44","EYP48","EYP27","EYP41"),
  Neuroticism  = c("EYP1","EYP5","EYP9","EYP13","EYP17","EYP21","EYP25","EYP30","EYP34","EYP38","EYP42","EYP46"),
  Lie          = c("EYP4","EYP16","EYP45","EYP8","EYP12","EYP20","EYP24","EYP29","EYP33","EYP37","EYP40","EYP47")
)

epq_scores <- tibble(
  Psychoticism = row_sum_safe(epq_items, epq_item_sets$Psychoticism),
  Extraversion = row_sum_safe(epq_items, epq_item_sets$Extraversion),
  Neuroticism  = row_sum_safe(epq_items, epq_item_sets$Neuroticism),
  Lie          = row_sum_safe(epq_items, epq_item_sets$Lie)
)

epq_core_scores <- epq_scores |> select(Psychoticism, Extraversion, Neuroticism)
```

```{r analysis-df, include=FALSE}
analysis_df <- bind_cols(
  demographics,
  bfi_domains |> rename_with(~ paste0(tolower(.x), "_bfi")),
  bfi_facets  |> rename_with(~ paste0(tolower(.x), "_bfi")),
  epq_scores  |> rename_with(~ paste0(tolower(.x), "_epq"))
) |> janitor::clean_names()
```

## Descriptive Statistics and Reliability

```{r descriptives, eval=FALSE, include=FALSE}
describe_tbl <- function(score_df, instrument, level) {
  psych::describe(score_df) |>
    as.data.frame() |>
    tibble::rownames_to_column("scale") |>
    as_tibble() |>
    transmute(instrument, level, scale, n, mean, sd, median, min, max, skew, kurtosis)
}

desc_table <- bind_rows(
  describe_tbl(bfi_domains, "BFI-2", "Domain"),
  describe_tbl(epq_scores, "EPQ-RS", "Scale")
) |> mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(.x, 3)))



describe_item_tbl <- function(item_df, instrument) {
  psych::describe(item_df) |>
    as.data.frame() |>
    tibble::rownames_to_column("item") |>
    as_tibble() |>
    transmute(
      instrument = instrument,
      level = "Item",
      item,
      n,
      mean,
      sd,
      median,
      min,
      max,
      skew,
      kurtosis
    )
}

epq_item_table_1 <- describe_item_tbl(epq_items, "EPQ-RS") |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(.x, 3)))
```

```{r GTfig, include=FALSE}
# =============================================================================
# EPQ-RS-B (48 items): Per-item descriptives + gtExtras in-cell graphics
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Adapted from a Likert-scale template. Key psychometric adjustments for
# dichotomous (Yes/No) EPQ-RS-B items:
#   * histogram (bw = 1) instead of density in the in-cell graphic
#   * Shapiro-Wilk removed (uninformative for binary data)
#   * corrected item-total r computed WITHIN subscale, not against a 48-item total
#   * Yes/No response pattern instead of 5-point Likert
#   * row groups P / E / N / L via groupname_col
# =============================================================================





# ---- 2. Subscale membership and row order -----------------------------------
epq_item_sets <- list(
  Psychoticism = c("EYP10","EYP14","EYP22","EYP31","EYP39","EYP2",
                   "EYP6","EYP18","EYP26","EYP28","EYP35","EYP43"),
  Extraversion = c("EYP3","EYP7","EYP11","EYP15","EYP19","EYP23",
                   "EYP32","EYP36","EYP44","EYP48","EYP27","EYP41"),
  Neuroticism  = c("EYP1","EYP5","EYP9","EYP13","EYP17","EYP21",
                   "EYP25","EYP30","EYP34","EYP38","EYP42","EYP46"),
  Lie          = c("EYP4","EYP16","EYP45","EYP8","EYP12","EYP20",
                   "EYP24","EYP29","EYP33","EYP37","EYP40","EYP47")
)
item_order <- unlist(epq_item_sets, use.names = FALSE)
gt.data    <- epq_items[, item_order]

# ---- 3. Per-item descriptives (binary-appropriate) --------------------------
psych_desc <- psych::describe(gt.data)
basic_desc <- tibble::tibble(
  Items = item_order,
  Mean  = round(psych_desc$mean,     2),   # = proportion endorsed (p) after keying
  SD    = round(psych_desc$sd,       2),
  Skew  = round(psych_desc$skew,     2),
  Kurt  = round(psych_desc$kurtosis, 2)
)

# ---- 4. Subscale-specific corrected item-total correlations -----------------
citc_one <- function(items_df) {
  k <- ncol(items_df)
  purrr::map_dbl(seq_len(k), function(j) {
    total_minus_j <- rowSums(items_df[, -j, drop = FALSE], na.rm = TRUE)
    suppressWarnings(stats::cor(items_df[[j]], total_minus_j,
                                use = "pairwise.complete.obs"))
  })
}
itc_tbl <- purrr::imap_dfr(epq_item_sets, function(cols, sub) {
  tibble::tibble(
    Items                    = cols,
    Subscale                 = sub,
    `Item-total correlation` = round(citc_one(epq_items[, cols]), 2)
  )
})

# ---- 5. Distribution data for the in-cell histogram -------------------------
hist_tbl <- tibble::tibble(
  Items     = item_order,
  hist_data = purrr::map(item_order, ~ gt.data[[.x]])
)


# ---- 6. Response-pattern percentages (Yes / No) -----------------------------
# Compute directly from the 0/1 keyed matrix; avoids kutils::likert
# column-naming inconsistencies across package versions.
pct_tbl <- purrr::imap_dfr(gt.data, function(vec, nm) {
  n_no  <- sum(vec == 0, na.rm = TRUE)
  n_yes <- sum(vec == 1, na.rm = TRUE)
  n_tot <- n_no + n_yes
  tibble::tibble(
    Items = nm,
    No    = sprintf("%.1f (%d)", 100 * n_no  / n_tot, n_no),
    Yes   = sprintf("%.1f (%d)", 100 * n_yes / n_tot, n_yes)
  )
})

# ---- 7. Assemble final table ------------------------------------------------
full_tbl <- basic_desc %>%
  dplyr::left_join(itc_tbl,  by = "Items") %>%
  dplyr::left_join(hist_tbl, by = "Items") %>%
  dplyr::left_join(pct_tbl,  by = "Items") %>%
  dplyr::mutate(Items = factor(Items, levels = item_order)) %>%
  dplyr::arrange(Items) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    Items    = as.character(Items),
    Subscale = factor(Subscale,
                      levels = c("Psychoticism","Extraversion",
                                 "Neuroticism","Lie"))
  ) %>%
  dplyr::select(Subscale, Items, Mean, SD, Skew, Kurt,
                `Item-total correlation`, hist_data, No, Yes)

dir.create("Outputs/Descriptives", recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)
readr::write_csv(
  full_tbl %>% dplyr::select(-hist_data),
  "Outputs/Descriptives/Descriptives_EPQ_RS_B.csv"
)

full_tbl <- full_tbl %>%
  dplyr::mutate(density_data = hist_data)


# ---- 8. gt / gtExtras figure 



# -- 2. Build the table --------------------------------------------------
gt_fig <- full_tbl %>%
  gt::gt(groupname_col = "Subscale", rowname_col = "Items") %>%
  gt::row_group_order(
    groups = c("Psychoticism", "Extraversion", "Neuroticism", "Lie")
  ) %>%
  gtExtras::gt_plt_dist(
    hist_data,
    type       = "histogram",
    line_color = "black",
    fill_color = "#00A08799",
    bw         = 1,
    same_limit = TRUE
  ) %>%
  gtExtras::gt_plt_dist(
    density_data,
    type       = "density",
    line_color = "black",
    fill_color = "#00A08799",
    same_limit = TRUE
  ) %>%
  gt::tab_header(
    title    = gt::md("**EPQ-RS-B Item Descriptives**"),
    subtitle = gt::md("*N* = 550; all items scored in the keyed 0/1 direction")
  ) %>%
  gt::tab_spanner(
    label   = "Summary Statistics",
    columns = c(Mean, SD, Skew, Kurt, `Item-total correlation`)
  ) %>%
  gt::tab_spanner(
    label   = "Graphics",
    columns = c(hist_data, density_data)
  ) %>%
  gt::tab_spanner(
    label   = "Response Pattern",
    columns = c(No, Yes)
  ) %>%
  gt::tab_footnote(
    footnote  = gt::md("Fisher's skewness and excess kurtosis."),
    locations = gt::cells_column_labels(columns = c(Skew, Kurt))
  ) %>%
  gt::tab_footnote(
    footnote  = gt::md("Corrected item-total *r* computed **within subscale**."),
    locations = gt::cells_column_labels(columns = `Item-total correlation`)
  ) %>%
  gt::tab_footnote(
    footnote  = gt::md("**% endorsed (n)**; Yes = keyed response."),
    locations = gt::cells_column_labels(columns = c(No, Yes))
  ) %>%
  gt::cols_label(
    Mean                     = gt::md("*M* (p\u208A)"),
    SD                       = gt::md("*SD*"),
    Skew                     = "Skew",
    Kurt                     = "Kurt",
    `Item-total correlation` = gt::md("*r* (item\u2013scale)"),
    hist_data                = "Histogram",
    density_data             = "Density",
    No                       = "No",
    Yes                      = "Yes"
  ) %>%
  gt::cols_align(
    align   = "center",
    columns = c(Mean, SD, Skew, Kurt, `Item-total correlation`, No, Yes)
  ) %>%
  gt::opt_align_table_header(align = "left") %>%
  gt::tab_options(
    # logical (CSS) sizes. These get multiplied by `zoom` on save.
    table.font.size        = gt::px(14L),
    row_group.font.weight  = "bold",
    data_row.padding       = gt::px(3L),
    column_labels.padding  = gt::px(4L),
    heading.padding        = gt::px(4L),
    row_group.padding      = gt::px(4L),
    footnotes.font.size    = gt::px(12L)
  )

# -- 3. Save at A4 width, 300 DPI ---------------------------------------
# A4 portrait width = 210 mm = 2480 px at 300 DPI.
# Using vwidth = 900 with zoom ≈ 2.76 gives ~2480 px output width.
# Height will flow naturally; expect ~5000 px (≈ 1.5 A4 pages tall).
gt::gtsave(
  gt_fig,
  "Outputs/figures/Descriptives_EPQ_RS_B.png",
  vwidth  = 900,
  vheight = 1800,
  zoom    = 2.76
)



# =======================================================================
# Per-subscale descriptives tables — one A4 portrait PNG per subscale
# =======================================================================



# -- 2. Output directory
out_dir <- "Outputs/figures"
if (!dir.exists(out_dir)) dir.create(out_dir, recursive = TRUE)

# -- 3. Loop over subscales
subscales <- c("Psychoticism", "Extraversion", "Neuroticism", "Lie")

for (sub in subscales) {

  tbl_sub <- full_tbl %>%
    dplyr::filter(Subscale == sub)

  n_items <- nrow(tbl_sub)

  gt_sub <- tbl_sub %>%
    gt::gt(rowname_col = "Items") %>%
    gtExtras::gt_plt_dist(
      hist_data,
      type       = "histogram",
      line_color = "black",
      fill_color = "#00A08799",
      bw         = 1,
      same_limit = TRUE
    ) %>%
    gtExtras::gt_plt_dist(
      density_data,
      type       = "density",
      line_color = "black",
      fill_color = "#00A08799",
      same_limit = TRUE
    ) %>%
    gt::tab_header(
      title    = gt::md(sprintf("**EPQ-RS-B Item Descriptives — %s**", sub)),
      subtitle = gt::md(sprintf(
        "*N* = 550; %d items scored in the keyed 0/1 direction",
        n_items
      ))
    ) %>%
    gt::tab_spanner(
      label   = "Summary Statistics",
      columns = c(Mean, SD, Skew, Kurt, `Item-total correlation`)
    ) %>%
    gt::tab_spanner(
      label   = "Graphics",
      columns = c(hist_data, density_data)
    ) %>%
    gt::tab_spanner(
      label   = "Response Pattern",
      columns = c(No, Yes)
    ) %>%
    gt::tab_footnote(
      footnote  = gt::md("Fisher's skewness and excess kurtosis."),
      locations = gt::cells_column_labels(columns = c(Skew, Kurt))
    ) %>%
    gt::tab_footnote(
      footnote  = gt::md("Corrected item-total *r* computed **within subscale**."),
      locations = gt::cells_column_labels(columns = `Item-total correlation`)
    ) %>%
    gt::tab_footnote(
      footnote  = gt::md("**% endorsed (n)**; Yes = keyed response."),
      locations = gt::cells_column_labels(columns = c(No, Yes))
    ) %>%
    gt::cols_hide(columns = Subscale) %>%
    gt::cols_label(
      Mean                     = gt::md("*M* (p\u208A)"),
      SD                       = gt::md("*SD*"),
      Skew                     = "Skew",
      Kurt                     = "Kurt",
      `Item-total correlation` = gt::md("*r* (item\u2013scale)"),
      hist_data                = "Histogram",
      density_data             = "Density",
      No                       = "No",
      Yes                      = "Yes"
    ) %>%
    gt::cols_align(
      align   = "center",
      columns = c(Mean, SD, Skew, Kurt, `Item-total correlation`, No, Yes)
    ) %>%
    gt::opt_align_table_header(align = "left") %>%
    gt::tab_options(
      table.font.size        = gt::px(18L),
      data_row.padding       = gt::px(5L),
      column_labels.padding  = gt::px(6L),
      heading.padding        = gt::px(6L),
      footnotes.font.size    = gt::px(14L),
      table.border.top.style    = "none",
      table.border.bottom.style = "none"
    )

  # -- 4. Save at A4 portrait, 300 DPI
  # A4 portrait: 210 mm × 297 mm = 2480 × 3508 px at 300 DPI.
  # vwidth = 900, zoom ≈ 2.76  ->  ~2480 px wide.
  # vheight flows naturally; with ~11 items the table sits well inside A4.
  gt::gtsave(
    gt_sub,
    filename = file.path(
      out_dir,
      sprintf("Descriptives_EPQ_RS_B_%s.png", sub)
    ),
    vwidth  = 900,
    vheight = 1400,
    zoom    = 2.76
  )
}

message("Saved ", length(subscales), " subscale figures to ", out_dir)

```


```{r tab-epqrsb-item-descriptives, results='asis'}

epq_item_table <- read_csv("outputs/Descriptives/Descriptives_EPQ_RS_B.csv")

epq_item_table_apa <- epq_item_table %>%
  transmute(
    Subscale,
    Items,
    Mean,
    SD,
    Skew,
    Kurt,
    `Item-total correlation`,
    No,
    Yes
  ) 
 

apa_table(
  epq_item_table_apa,
  caption = "Item-level descriptive statistics for the 48 items of the Bangla EPQ-RS.",
  note = "No and Yes indicate the proportions or percentages endorsing each response category, depending on how the table was created."
)
```


```{r reliability}
compute_reliability <- function(item_data, item_sets, instrument, level, item_type = c("ordinal", "binary")) {
  item_type <- match.arg(item_type)
  purrr::map_dfr(names(item_sets), function(sc) {
    dat <- item_data[, item_sets[[sc]], drop = FALSE]
    tibble(
      instrument = instrument, level = level, scale = sc, n_items = ncol(dat),
      alpha = safe_alpha(dat),
      ordinal_alpha = safe_ordinal_alpha(dat, item_type = item_type),
      omega_total = safe_omega_total(dat, item_type = item_type)
    )
  })
}

reliability_table <- bind_rows(
  compute_reliability(bfi_items, bfi_domain_items, "BFI-2", "Domain", item_type = "ordinal"),
  compute_reliability(epq_items, epq_item_sets, "EPQ-RS", "Scale", item_type = "binary")
) |> mutate(across(c(alpha, ordinal_alpha, omega_total), ~ round(.x, 3)))
```

Table \@ref(tab:rel-table) presents internal consistency estimates for all scales. For the EPQ-RS-B, Extraversion and Neuroticism achieved acceptable reliability across all three estimators. The Lie scale was adequate. Psychoticism, consistent with prior cross-cultural findings, was markedly weaker.

```{r rel-table, results='asis'}
rel_epq <- reliability_table |> filter(instrument == "EPQ-RS")
apa_table(
  rel_epq |> select(Scale = scale, Items = n_items, `$\\alpha$` = alpha,
                     `Ordinal $\\alpha$` = ordinal_alpha, `$\\omega_t$` = omega_total),
  caption = "Internal consistency estimates for the EPQ-RS-B subscales.",
  note = "$\\alpha$ = Cronbach's alpha (KR-20 equivalent for dichotomous items); Ordinal $\\alpha$ = alpha from tetrachoric correlations; $\\omega_t$ = McDonald's omega total.",
  escape = FALSE
)
```

## Structural Validity

```{r epq-esem, include=FALSE}
# ============================================================
# EPQ ESEM: 3-factor and 4-factor models
# ============================================================


# --- Item selection (must match target block counts) ---
epq_items_3f <- epq_items %>%
  select(
    # Psychoticism (12)
    EYP10, EYP14, EYP22, EYP31, EYP39, EYP2, EYP6, EYP18, EYP26, EYP28, EYP35, EYP43,
    # Extraversion (12)
    EYP3, EYP7, EYP11, EYP15, EYP19, EYP23, EYP32, EYP36, EYP44, EYP48, EYP27, EYP41,
    # Neuroticism (12)
    EYP1, EYP5, EYP9, EYP13, EYP17, EYP21, EYP25, EYP30, EYP34, EYP38, EYP42, EYP46
  )

epq_items_4f <- epq_items_3f %>%
  bind_cols(
    epq_items %>% select(
      EYP4, EYP16, EYP45, EYP8, EYP12, EYP20, EYP24, EYP29, EYP33, EYP37, EYP40, EYP47
    )
  )

# --- 3-FACTOR ESEM ---
target_3f <- make_target(
  nitems = 36,
  mainloadings = list(
    Psychoticism = 1:12,
    Extraversion = 13:24,
    Neuroticism  = 25:36
  )
)

efa_3f <- esem_efa(
  data     = epq_items_3f,
  nfactors = 3,
  target   = target_3f,
  fm       = "uls",
  cor      = "tet"
)

ref_3f   <- find_referents(efa_3f, factor_names = c("Neuroticism", "Extraversion", "Psychoticism"))
syn_3f   <- syntax_composer(efa_3f, ref_3f)

epq_items_3f_ord <- epq_items_3f %>% mutate(across(everything(), ~ ordered(.x)))

epq_fit_3 <- cfa(
  model            = syn_3f,
  data             = epq_items_3f_ord,
  ordered          = names(epq_items_3f_ord),
  estimator        = "WLSMV",
  parameterization = "theta",
  mimic            = "Mplus",
  std.lv           = TRUE
)

# --- 4-FACTOR ESEM ---
target_4f <- make_target(
  nitems = 48,
  mainloadings = list(
    Psychoticism = 1:12,
    Extraversion = 13:24,
    Neuroticism  = 25:36,
    Lie          = 37:48
  )
)

efa_4f <- esem_efa(
  data     = epq_items_4f,
  nfactors = 4,
  target   = target_4f,
  fm       = "uls",
  cor      = "tet"
)

ref_4f   <- find_referents(efa_4f, factor_names = c("Neuroticism", "Extraversion", "Lie", "Psychoticism"))
syn_4f   <- syntax_composer(efa_4f, ref_4f)

epq_items_4f_ord <- epq_items_4f %>% mutate(across(everything(), ~ ordered(.x)))

epq_fit_4 <- cfa(
  model            = syn_4f,
  data             = epq_items_4f_ord,
  ordered          = names(epq_items_4f_ord),
  estimator        = "WLSMV",
  parameterization = "theta",
  mimic            = "Mplus",
  std.lv           = TRUE
)

# --- Fit comparison table ---
epq_fit_table <- bind_rows(
  tibble(
    model = "3-factor ESEM (P, E, N)",
    chi_sq = sprintf("%.2f (%d)*", fitMeasures(epq_fit_3, "chisq.scaled"),
                     fitMeasures(epq_fit_3, "df.scaled")),
    # p      = round(fitMeasures(epq_fit_3, "pvalue.scaled"), 3),
    CFI    = round(fitMeasures(epq_fit_3, "cfi.scaled"), 3),
    TLI    = round(fitMeasures(epq_fit_3, "tli.scaled"), 3),
    RMSEA  = round(fitMeasures(epq_fit_3, "rmsea.scaled"), 3),
    RMSEA_CI = sprintf("[%.3f, %.3f]",
                       fitMeasures(epq_fit_3, "rmsea.ci.lower.scaled"),
                       fitMeasures(epq_fit_3, "rmsea.ci.upper.scaled")),
    SRMR   = round(fitMeasures(epq_fit_3, "srmr"), 3)
  ),
  tibble(
    model = "4-factor ESEM (P, E, N, L)",
    chi_sq = sprintf("%.2f (%d)*", fitMeasures(epq_fit_4, "chisq.scaled"),
                     fitMeasures(epq_fit_4, "df.scaled")),
    # p      = round(fitMeasures(epq_fit_4, "pvalue.scaled"), 3),
    CFI    = round(fitMeasures(epq_fit_4, "cfi.scaled"), 3),
    TLI    = round(fitMeasures(epq_fit_4, "tli.scaled"), 3),
    RMSEA  = round(fitMeasures(epq_fit_4, "rmsea.scaled"), 3),
    RMSEA_CI = sprintf("[%.3f, %.3f]",
                       fitMeasures(epq_fit_4, "rmsea.ci.lower.scaled"),
                       fitMeasures(epq_fit_4, "rmsea.ci.upper.scaled")),
    SRMR   = round(fitMeasures(epq_fit_4, "srmr"), 3)
  )
)





# --- 3-factor ESEM loadings ---
epq_esem_loadings_3f <- parameterEstimates(epq_fit_3, standardized = TRUE) %>%
  filter(op == "=~") %>%
  transmute(Item = rhs, Factor = lhs, Loading = round(std.all, 3)) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = Factor, values_from = Loading)

# --- 4-factor ESEM loadings ---
epq_esem_loadings_4f <- parameterEstimates(epq_fit_4, standardized = TRUE) %>%
  filter(op == "=~") %>%
  transmute(Item = rhs, Factor = lhs, Loading = round(std.all, 3)) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = Factor, values_from = Loading)

suppress <- function(df, threshold = .10) {
  df %>% mutate(across(-Item, ~ ifelse(abs(.x) < threshold, NA_real_, .x)))
}

epq_esem_loadings_3f_sup <- suppress(epq_esem_loadings_3f)
epq_esem_loadings_4f_sup <- suppress(epq_esem_loadings_4f)

readr::write_csv(epq_esem_loadings_3f_sup, "outputs/ESEM/epq_esem_loadings_3factor_suppressed.csv")
readr::write_csv(epq_esem_loadings_4f_sup, "outputs/ESEM/epq_esem_loadings_4factor_suppressed.csv")


```

Table \@ref(tab:cfa-fit) presents ESEM fit indices for the EPQ-RS-B. Both 3-factor and 4-factor models were fitted using WLSMV estimation.

```{r cfa-fit, results='asis'}
apa_table(
  epq_fit_table,
  caption = "Confirmatory factor analysis fit indices for the EPQ-RS-B.",
  note = "WLSMV estimation with items treated as ordered categorical."
)
```


## Measurement Invariance-Sex
The configural model (M1) provided adequate baseline fit (CFI = .965, TLI = .958, RMSEA = .019), confirming that the four-factor structure of the EPQ-RS-B was tenable in both sex groups. Adding metric constraints (M2) produced no deterioration in fit; indeed, CFI increased slightly to .970 and RMSEA decreased to .017 (ΔCFI = +.005, ΔRMSEA = −.002), indicating that factor loadings were equivalent across groups. Models incorporating residual variance constraints (M3), factor variance/covariance constraints (M4), and their combination (M6) similarly met or exceeded baseline fit levels, with CFI values ranging from .970 to .976 and RMSEA from .015 to .017.
The introduction of scalar (intercept) constraints at M5 produced a modest decrement in CFI relative to the metric model (CFI = .965 vs. .970; ΔCFI = −.005), though this change remained well within the −.01 threshold. Models combining scalar constraints with residual and/or factor variance/covariance constraints (M7 to M9) showed comparable fit (CFI = .966 to .972, RMSEA = .016 to .018), all satisfying the ΔCFI and ΔRMSEA criteria.
The most notable strain emerged at M10 (scalar + factor means), where CFI dropped to .943 (ΔCFI = −.022 relative to M5), accompanied by a rise in RMSEA to .023. This decrement exceeded the conventional −.01 threshold and signals that constraining latent means to equality across sexes introduced appreciable misfit. Models M11 and M12, which added residual or factor variance/covariance constraints on top of M10, showed similar levels of strain (CFI = .942 to .945).
However, the fully constrained model M13 (scalar + residuals + factor variances/covariances + latent means) recovered fit to an acceptable level (CFI = .950, RMSEA = .021), yielding ΔCFI = +.002 and ΔRMSEA = −.001 relative to M10. This pattern, in which the terminal model satisfies practical fit-change criteria despite localized strain at an intermediate step, supports the conclusion that full measurement invariance is tenable at the most restrictive endpoint of the taxonomy.

```{r MI, include=FALSE}
# ============================================================
# EPQ ESEM Invariance Testing (Marsh 13-model taxonomy)
# ============================================================

# --- Invariance data ---
epq_invariance_data <- tibble(
  Gender = factor(demographics$gender)
) %>%
  bind_cols(as.data.frame(epq_items))

epq_ordered_items <- names(epq_items)

# --- Use 4-factor ESEM syntax ---
epq_invariance_model <- syn_4f

# --- Marsh 13 specs ---
SCALAR_PARAM <- "thresholds"

epq_marsh13_equal <- list(
  M1  = NULL,
  M2  = c("loadings"),
  M3  = c("loadings", "residuals"),
  M4  = c("loadings", "lv.variances", "lv.covariances"),
  M5  = c("loadings", SCALAR_PARAM),
  M6  = c("loadings", "residuals", "lv.variances", "lv.covariances"),
  M7  = c("loadings", SCALAR_PARAM, "residuals"),
  M8  = c("loadings", SCALAR_PARAM, "lv.variances", "lv.covariances"),
  M9  = c("loadings", SCALAR_PARAM, "residuals", "lv.variances", "lv.covariances"),
  M10 = c("loadings", SCALAR_PARAM, "means"),
  M11 = c("loadings", SCALAR_PARAM, "residuals", "means"),
  M12 = c("loadings", SCALAR_PARAM, "lv.variances", "lv.covariances", "means"),
  M13 = c("loadings", SCALAR_PARAM, "residuals", "lv.variances", "lv.covariances", "means")
)

epq_model_partial    <- setNames(vector("list", 13), paste0("M", 1:13))
epq_model_extra_args <- setNames(vector("list", 13), paste0("M", 1:13))

# --- Fitting function ---
fit_epq_M <- function(id) {
  base_args <- list(
    model            = epq_invariance_model,
    data             = epq_invariance_data,
    group            = "Gender",
    ordered          = epq_ordered_items,
    estimator        = "WLSMV",
    parameterization = "theta",
    std.lv           = TRUE,
    meanstructure    = TRUE,
    group.equal      = epq_marsh13_equal[[id]],
    group.partial    = epq_model_partial[[id]]
  )
  args <- modifyList(base_args, epq_model_extra_args[[id]] %||% list())
  do.call(lavaan::cfa, args)
}

# --- Fit all 13 models ---
epq_models <- setNames(
  lapply(paste0("M", 1:13), function(id) {
    message("Fitting EPQ ", id)
    fit_epq_M(id)
  }),
  paste0("M", 1:13)
)

.pick <- function(fm, primary, fallback) {
  if (!is.null(fm[[primary]])) unname(fm[[primary]])
  else if (!is.null(fm[[fallback]])) unname(fm[[fallback]])
  else NA_real_
}

# --- Extract fit indices ---
epq_fit_tbl <- bind_rows(lapply(names(epq_models), function(id) {
  fit <- epq_models[[id]]
  conv <- tryCatch(lavInspect(fit, "converged"), error = function(e) NA)
  fm   <- tryCatch(fitMeasures(fit), error = function(e) NULL)
  if (is.null(fm)) {
    return(tibble(Model = id, converged = conv,
                  Chi_Square = NA_real_, df = NA_real_,
                  CFI = NA_real_, TLI = NA_real_, RMSEA = NA_real_,
                  RMSEA_90_L = NA_real_, RMSEA_90_U = NA_real_, SRMR = NA_real_))
  }
  tibble(
    Model      = id,
    converged  = conv,
    Chi_Square = .pick(fm, "chisq.scaled", "chisq"),
    df         = .pick(fm, "df.scaled", "df"),
    CFI        = .pick(fm, "cfi.scaled", "cfi"),
    TLI        = .pick(fm, "tli.scaled", "tli"),
    RMSEA      = .pick(fm, "rmsea.scaled", "rmsea"),
    RMSEA_90_L = .pick(fm, "rmsea.ci.lower.scaled", "rmsea.ci.lower"),
    RMSEA_90_U = .pick(fm, "rmsea.ci.upper.scaled", "rmsea.ci.upper"),
    SRMR       = .pick(fm, "srmr", "srmr")
  )
})) %>%
  mutate(Model = factor(Model, levels = paste0("M", 1:13))) %>%
  arrange(Model)

# --- Sequential LRT ---

safe_LRT <- function(prev, curr) {
  tab <- tryCatch(lavTestLRT(prev, curr), error = function(e) NULL)
  if (is.null(tab)) return(c(chisq_diff = NA_real_, df_diff = NA_real_, p = NA_real_))
  tab <- as.data.frame(tab)
  i <- nrow(tab)
  chisq <- if ("Chisq diff" %in% names(tab)) tab[["Chisq diff"]][i] else NA_real_
  df    <- if ("Df diff"    %in% names(tab)) tab[["Df diff"]][i]    else NA_real_
  p     <- if ("Pr(>Chisq)" %in% names(tab)) tab[["Pr(>Chisq)"]][i] else NA_real_
  c(chisq_diff = as.numeric(chisq), df_diff = as.numeric(df), p = as.numeric(p))
}


epq_diff_tbl <- tibble(
  Model = paste0("M", 1:13),
  chisq_diff = NA_real_, df_diff = NA_real_, p = NA_real_
)

for (i in 2:13) {
  d <- safe_LRT(epq_models[[i - 1]], epq_models[[i]])
  epq_diff_tbl$chisq_diff[i] <- d[["chisq_diff"]]
  epq_diff_tbl$df_diff[i]    <- d[["df_diff"]]
  epq_diff_tbl$p[i]          <- d[["p"]]
}

# --- Combined table with delta criteria ---
epq_invariance_table <- epq_fit_tbl %>%
  left_join(epq_diff_tbl, by = "Model") %>%
  mutate(
    dCFI   = CFI   - lag(CFI),
    dTLI   = TLI   - lag(TLI),
    dRMSEA = RMSEA - lag(RMSEA),
    CFI_invariance   = dCFI >= -0.01,
    RMSEA_invariance = dRMSEA <= 0.01
  )

# --- Pretty APA table ---
epq_invariance_pretty <- epq_invariance_table %>%
  transmute(
    Model,
    `Chi-Square`             = Chi_Square,
    df,
    CFI, TLI, RMSEA,
    `RMSEA 90% Lower CI`     = RMSEA_90_L,
    `RMSEA 90% Upper`        = RMSEA_90_U,
    `Chi-Square Difference`  = chisq_diff,
    `df difference*`         = df_diff,
    p,
    CFI_diff                 = dCFI,
    TLI_diff                 = dTLI,
    RMSEA_diff               = dRMSEA,
    CFI_invariance,
    RMSEA_invariance,
    converged
  )


fmt2 <- function(x) ifelse(is.na(x), "", sprintf("%.2f", x))
fmt3 <- function(x) ifelse(is.na(x), "", sprintf("%.3f", x))
apa_p <- function(p) {
  ifelse(
    is.na(p),
    "",
    ifelse(
      p < .001,
      "< .001",
      sub("^0", "", sprintf("%.3f", p))
    )
  )
}

# --- Pretty APA table ---
epq_invariance_pretty <- epq_invariance_table %>%
  transmute(
    Model,
    `Chi-Square`            = Chi_Square,
    df,
    CFI,
    TLI,
    RMSEA,
    `RMSEA 90% Lower CI`    = RMSEA_90_L,
    `RMSEA 90% Upper`       = RMSEA_90_U,
    `Chi-Square Difference` = chisq_diff,
    `df difference*`        = df_diff,
    p,
    CFI_diff                = dCFI,
    TLI_diff                = dTLI,
    RMSEA_diff              = dRMSEA,
    CFI_invariance,
    RMSEA_invariance,
    converged
  )

# --- Formatting helpers ---
fmt2 <- function(x) ifelse(is.na(x), "", sprintf("%.2f", x))
fmt3 <- function(x) ifelse(is.na(x), "", sprintf("%.3f", x))

apa_p <- function(p) {
  ifelse(
    is.na(p),
    "",
    ifelse(
      p < .001,
      "p < .001",
      paste0( sub("^0", "", sprintf("%.3f", p)))
    )
  )
}


# --- Final table formatter ---
prep_invariance_tbl <- function(df, model_col = "Model") {

  m13_labels <- c(
    M1  = "M1 (Configural)",
    M2  = "M2 (Metric)",
    M3  = "M3 (Metric + Residuals)",
    M4  = "M4 (Metric + Factor Var/Cov)",
    M5  = "M5 (Scalar)",
    M6  = "M6 (Metric + Residuals + Factor Var/Cov)",
    M7  = "M7 (Scalar + Residuals)",
    M8  = "M8 (Scalar + Factor Var/Cov)",
    M9  = "M9 (Scalar + Residuals + Factor Var/Cov)",
    M10 = "M10 (Scalar + Means)",
    M11 = "M11 (Scalar + Residuals + Means)",
    M12 = "M12 (Scalar + Factor Var/Cov + Means)",
    M13 = "M13 (Scalar + Residuals + Factor Var/Cov + Means)"
  )

  df <- df %>%
    mutate(
      Model_disp = if ("Model_Label" %in% names(.)) {
        .data[["Model_Label"]]
      } else {
        .data[[model_col]]
      },
      Model_disp = dplyr::recode(Model_disp, !!!as.list(m13_labels), .default = Model_disp),
      RMSEA_CI = ifelse(
        is.na(.data[["RMSEA 90% Lower CI"]]) | is.na(.data[["RMSEA 90% Upper"]]),
        "",
        paste0(
          "[",
          fmt3(.data[["RMSEA 90% Lower CI"]]),
          ", ",
          fmt3(.data[["RMSEA 90% Upper"]]),
          "]"
        )
      )
    )

  df %>%
    transmute(
      Model      = Model_disp,
      `χ²`       = fmt2(.data[["Chi-Square"]]),
      df         = ifelse(is.na(.data[["df"]]), "", as.character(as.integer(.data[["df"]]))),
      CFI        = fmt3(.data[["CFI"]]),
      TLI        = fmt3(.data[["TLI"]]),
      RMSEA      = fmt3(.data[["RMSEA"]]),
      `RMSEA 90% CI` = RMSEA_CI,
      `Δχ²`      = fmt2(.data[["Chi-Square Difference"]]),
      `Δdf`      = ifelse(
        is.na(.data[["df difference*"]]),
        "",
        as.character(as.integer(.data[["df difference*"]]))
      ),
      p          = apa_p(.data[["p"]]),
      `ΔCFI`     = fmt3(.data[["CFI_diff"]]),
      `ΔTLI`     = fmt3(.data[["TLI_diff"]]),
      `ΔRMSEA`   = fmt3(.data[["RMSEA_diff"]]),
      CFI_inv    = ifelse(is.na(.data[["CFI_invariance"]]), "", ifelse(.data[["CFI_invariance"]], "Yes", "No")),
      RMSEA_inv  = ifelse(is.na(.data[["RMSEA_invariance"]]), "", ifelse(.data[["RMSEA_invariance"]], "Yes", "No")),
      Converged  = ifelse(is.na(.data[["converged"]]), "", ifelse(.data[["converged"]], "Yes", "No"))
    )
}

# --- Create final APA-ready table ---
epq_invariance_apa <- prep_invariance_tbl(epq_invariance_pretty)
readr::write_csv(epq_invariance_pretty, "outputs/tables/EYP_invariance_APA.csv")
```

```{r genderMItab}
papaja::apa_table(
  epq_invariance_pretty,
  caption = "Measurement invariance results for the EPQ-RS-B.",
  note = "Δ values are relative to the immediately preceding model. * p < .05, ** p < .01, *** p < .001."
)
```



## Measurement Invariance-Language



```{r MIUK, eval=FALSE, include=FALSE}
# ============================================================
# BUILD 44-ITEM LANGUAGE INVARIANCE DATA
# English source: England-total sample UK scored EPQ item data.xls
# Bangla source: epq_items (must already be keyed 0/1 in the same direction)
# ============================================================

library(dplyr)
library(tibble)
library(purrr)
library(readxl)
library(readr)

# ------------------------------------------------------------
# 1. Verified 44 common items between 90-item EPQ and 48-item EPQ-RS
#    Excluded short-form-only items: EYP2, EYP14, EYP26, EYP43
# ------------------------------------------------------------
common_item_map <- tibble::tribble(
  ~EYP,    ~epq90_item, ~scale,
  "EYP1",    3,  "Neuroticism",
  "EYP3",    5,  "Extraversion",
  "EYP4",   13,  "Lie",
  "EYP5",    7,  "Neuroticism",
  "EYP6",    6,  "Psychoticism",
  "EYP7",   10,  "Extraversion",
  "EYP8",    8,  "Lie",
  "EYP9",   15,  "Neuroticism",
  "EYP10",  22,  "Psychoticism",
  "EYP11",  17,  "Extraversion",
  "EYP12",  16,  "Lie",
  "EYP13",  19,  "Neuroticism",
  "EYP15",  14,  "Extraversion",
  "EYP16",  20,  "Lie",
  "EYP17",  23,  "Neuroticism",
  "EYP18",  37,  "Psychoticism",
  "EYP19",  40,  "Extraversion",
  "EYP20",  24,  "Lie",
  "EYP21",  31,  "Neuroticism",
  "EYP22",  43,  "Psychoticism",
  "EYP23",  45,  "Extraversion",
  "EYP24",  39,  "Lie",
  "EYP25",  34,  "Neuroticism",
  "EYP27",  21,  "Extraversion",
  "EYP28",  53,  "Psychoticism",
  "EYP29",  48,  "Lie",
  "EYP30",  41,  "Neuroticism",
  "EYP31",  67,  "Psychoticism",
  "EYP32",  52,  "Extraversion",
  "EYP33",  51,  "Lie",
  "EYP34",  72,  "Neuroticism",
  "EYP35",  71,  "Psychoticism",
  "EYP36",  82,  "Extraversion",
  "EYP37",  59,  "Lie",
  "EYP38",  75,  "Neuroticism",
  "EYP39",  83,  "Psychoticism",
  "EYP40",  63,  "Lie",
  "EYP41",  42,  "Extraversion",
  "EYP42",  77,  "Neuroticism",
  "EYP44",  86,  "Extraversion",
  "EYP45",  78,  "Lie",
  "EYP46",  27,  "Neuroticism",
  "EYP47",  85,  "Lie",
  "EYP48",  70,  "Extraversion"
)

common_items <- common_item_map$EYP

# ------------------------------------------------------------
# 2. 44-item subscale definitions
# ------------------------------------------------------------
epq_common_item_sets <- list(
  Psychoticism = c("EYP10","EYP22","EYP31","EYP39","EYP6","EYP18","EYP28","EYP35"),
  Extraversion = c("EYP3","EYP7","EYP11","EYP15","EYP19","EYP23","EYP32","EYP36","EYP44","EYP48","EYP27","EYP41"),
  Neuroticism  = c("EYP1","EYP5","EYP9","EYP13","EYP17","EYP21","EYP25","EYP30","EYP34","EYP38","EYP42","EYP46"),
  Lie          = c("EYP4","EYP8","EYP12","EYP16","EYP20","EYP24","EYP29","EYP33","EYP37","EYP40","EYP45","EYP47")
)

# ------------------------------------------------------------
# 3. Helper
# ------------------------------------------------------------
row_sum_safe <- function(df, cols) {
  out <- rowSums(df[, cols, drop = FALSE], na.rm = TRUE)
  all_missing <- rowSums(!is.na(df[, cols, drop = FALSE])) == 0
  out[all_missing] <- NA_real_
  out
}

# ------------------------------------------------------------
# 4. Map original EPQ item numbers to UK-scored column names
#    UK-scored files are already keyed and stored in SCALE ORDER.
#    So do NOT use positional extraction from a binary block.
# ------------------------------------------------------------
prefix_map <- c(
  Psychoticism = "P",
  Extraversion = "E",
  Neuroticism  = "N",
  Lie          = "L"
)

common_item_map <- common_item_map %>%
  mutate(
    uk_col = paste0(unname(prefix_map[scale]), "_", epq90_item)
  )

# ------------------------------------------------------------
# 5. Read England UK-scored spreadsheet
#    Change the path if needed in your project
# ------------------------------------------------------------
eng_file <- "EPQ- Invariance/Analysis-Files/UKscored//Total/Excel/England-total sample UK scored EPQ item data.xls"

eng_raw <- readxl::read_excel(eng_file, sheet = 1)
names(eng_raw) <- trimws(names(eng_raw))

# ------------------------------------------------------------
# 6. Check required item columns exist
# ------------------------------------------------------------
missing_cols <- setdiff(common_item_map$uk_col, names(eng_raw))

if (length(missing_cols) > 0) {
  stop(
    "These expected UK-scored item columns were not found in the England file:\n",
    paste(missing_cols, collapse = ", ")
  )
}

# ------------------------------------------------------------
# 7. Extract the 44 common UK-English items
#    IMPORTANT:
#    This spreadsheet is already UK-scored, so do NOT reverse-code again
# ------------------------------------------------------------
uk_english_common <- tibble(ID = seq_len(nrow(eng_raw))) %>%
  bind_cols(
    purrr::map_dfc(seq_len(nrow(common_item_map)), function(i) {
      src <- common_item_map$uk_col[i]
      dst <- common_item_map$EYP[i]
      tibble(!!dst := suppressWarnings(as.numeric(eng_raw[[src]])))
    })
  ) %>%
  filter(if_all(all_of(common_items), ~ !is.na(.x))) %>%
  mutate(
    Language = "UK-English",
    .before = 1
  ) %>%
  select(Language, ID, all_of(common_items))

# ------------------------------------------------------------
# 8. Extract the 44 common Bangla items
#    epq_items must already be keyed in the same 0/1 direction
# ------------------------------------------------------------
bangla_common <- epq_items %>%
  select(all_of(common_items)) %>%
  mutate(
    Language = "Bangla",
    ID = seq_len(n()),
    .before = 1
  ) %>%
  select(Language, ID, all_of(common_items))

# ------------------------------------------------------------
# 9. Build combined language invariance data
# ------------------------------------------------------------
epq_language44_data_uk <- bind_rows(uk_english_common, bangla_common) %>%
  mutate(Language = factor(Language, levels = c("UK-English", "Bangla")))

epq_language44_data_ord_uk <- epq_language44_data_uk %>%
  mutate(across(all_of(common_items), ~ ordered(.x, levels = c(0, 1))))

# ------------------------------------------------------------
# 10. Optional 44-item scale scores
# ------------------------------------------------------------
epq_language44_scores <- epq_language44_data_uk %>%
  transmute(
    Language,
    ID,
    Psychoticism = row_sum_safe(., epq_common_item_sets$Psychoticism),
    Extraversion = row_sum_safe(., epq_common_item_sets$Extraversion),
    Neuroticism  = row_sum_safe(., epq_common_item_sets$Neuroticism),
    Lie          = row_sum_safe(., epq_common_item_sets$Lie)
  )

epq_language44_core_scores <- epq_language44_scores %>%
  select(Language, ID, Psychoticism, Extraversion, Neuroticism)

# ------------------------------------------------------------
# 11. Save
# ------------------------------------------------------------
saveRDS(
  epq_language44_data_uk,
  "outputs/Language Invariance/epq_language44_data_from_England_UKscored_EPQ.rds"
)

saveRDS(
  epq_language44_data_ord_uk,
  "outputs/Language Invariance/epq_language44_data_from_England_UKscored_EPQ_ordered.rds"
)

readr::write_csv(
  common_item_map,
  "outputs/Language Invariance/epq_language44_item_map_England_UKscored.csv"
)

readr::write_csv(
  epq_language44_scores,
  "outputs/Language Invariance/epq_language44_scores_England_UKscored.csv"
)

# ------------------------------------------------------------
# 12. Quick checks
# ------------------------------------------------------------
table(epq_language44_data_uk$Language)

colSums(is.na(epq_language44_data_uk[, common_items, drop = FALSE]))

glimpse(epq_language44_data_uk)
```

```{r MIUKMI}
# ============================================================
# EPQ ESEM LANGUAGE INVARIANCE TESTING (44 common items)
# UK-English vs Bangla
# Marsh 13-model taxonomy + progress bar
# Separate UK-specific version
# ============================================================

# NOTE:
# This script expects the following objects to be available in the
# workspace before it is sourced:
#   * epq_items          data.frame of Bangla EPQ items (used for EFA)
# and the following packages loaded:
#   * dplyr, tibble, readr, lavaan, esemComp

# ------------------------------------------------------------
# 1. Read 44-item UK language invariance data
# ------------------------------------------------------------
# epq_language_data_uk_raw <- readRDS(
#   "outputs/Language Invariance/epq_language44_data_from_England_UKscored_EPQ.rds"
# )

epq_language_data_uk <- readRDS(
  "outputs/Language Invariance/epq_language44_data_from_England_UKscored_EPQ_ordered.rds"
)

# 44 common items (EYP2, EYP14, EYP26, EYP43 excluded)
common_items <- c(
  "EYP1","EYP3","EYP4","EYP5","EYP6","EYP7","EYP8","EYP9","EYP10","EYP11",
  "EYP12","EYP13","EYP15","EYP16","EYP17","EYP18","EYP19","EYP20","EYP21",
  "EYP22","EYP23","EYP24","EYP25","EYP27","EYP28","EYP29","EYP30","EYP31",
  "EYP32","EYP33","EYP34","EYP35","EYP36","EYP37","EYP38","EYP39","EYP40",
  "EYP41","EYP42","EYP44","EYP45","EYP46","EYP47","EYP48"
)

epq_ordered_items <- common_items

epq_common_item_sets <- list(
  Psychoticism = c("EYP10","EYP22","EYP31","EYP39","EYP6","EYP18","EYP28","EYP35"),
  Extraversion = c("EYP3","EYP7","EYP11","EYP15","EYP19","EYP23","EYP32","EYP36","EYP44","EYP48","EYP27","EYP41"),
  Neuroticism  = c("EYP1","EYP5","EYP9","EYP13","EYP17","EYP21","EYP25","EYP30","EYP34","EYP38","EYP42","EYP46"),
  Lie          = c("EYP4","EYP8","EYP12","EYP16","EYP20","EYP24","EYP29","EYP33","EYP37","EYP40","EYP45","EYP47")
)

# ------------------------------------------------------------
# 2. Sanity checks
# ------------------------------------------------------------
if (!exists("epq_items")) {
  stop("`epq_items` not found. Load the Bangla EPQ item data before sourcing this script.")
}

if (!"Language" %in% names(epq_language_data_uk)) {
  stop("`Language` column not found in epq_language_data_uk.")
}

expected_groups <- c("UK-English", "Bangla")
found_groups <- levels(factor(epq_language_data_uk$Language))

if (!all(expected_groups %in% found_groups)) {
  warning(
    "Expected Language groups: ",
    paste(expected_groups, collapse = ", "),
    ". Found: ",
    paste(found_groups, collapse = ", ")
  )
}

# ------------------------------------------------------------
# 3. Build 44-item Bangla data for EFA
# ------------------------------------------------------------
epq_items_4f_44 <- epq_items %>%
  dplyr::select(
    # Psychoticism (8)
    EYP10, EYP22, EYP31, EYP39, EYP6, EYP18, EYP28, EYP35,
    # Extraversion (12)
    EYP3, EYP7, EYP11, EYP15, EYP19, EYP23, EYP32, EYP36, EYP44, EYP48, EYP27, EYP41,
    # Neuroticism (12)
    EYP1, EYP5, EYP9, EYP13, EYP17, EYP21, EYP25, EYP30, EYP34, EYP38, EYP42, EYP46,
    # Lie (12)
    EYP4, EYP16, EYP45, EYP8, EYP12, EYP20, EYP24, EYP29, EYP33, EYP37, EYP40, EYP47
  )

# ------------------------------------------------------------
# 4. Target matrix and ESEM-EFA
# ------------------------------------------------------------
target_4f_44 <- make_target(
  nitems       = 44,
  mainloadings = list(
    Psychoticism = 1:8,
    Extraversion = 9:20,
    Neuroticism  = 21:32,
    Lie          = 33:44
  )
)

efa_4f_44_uk <- esem_efa(
  data     = epq_items_4f_44,
  nfactors = 4,
  target   = target_4f_44,
  fm       = "uls",
  cor      = "tet"
)

ref_4f_44_uk <- find_referents(
  efa_4f_44_uk,
  factor_names = c("Neuroticism", "Extraversion", "Lie", "Psychoticism")
)

syn_4f_44_uk <- syntax_composer(efa_4f_44_uk, ref_4f_44_uk)

# lavaan model syntax used downstream
epq_invariance_model_uk <- syn_4f_44_uk

# ------------------------------------------------------------
# 5. Marsh 13 invariance specifications
# ------------------------------------------------------------
SCALAR_PARAM <- "thresholds"

epq_marsh13_equal_uk <- list(
  M1  = NULL,
  M2  = c("loadings"),
  M3  = c("loadings", "residuals"),
  M4  = c("loadings", "lv.variances", "lv.covariances"),
  M5  = c("loadings", SCALAR_PARAM),
  M6  = c("loadings", "residuals", "lv.variances", "lv.covariances"),
  M7  = c("loadings", SCALAR_PARAM, "residuals"),
  M8  = c("loadings", SCALAR_PARAM, "lv.variances", "lv.covariances"),
```
