```{r 2PLDIF, include=FALSE}
  Item      = neu_nonuniform_df$Item,
  X2        = neu_nonuniform_df$X2,
  df        = neu_nonuniform_df$df,
  p         = neu_nonuniform_df$p,
  adj_p     = neu_nonuniform_df$adj_p,
  DIF_flag  = ifelse(!is.na(neu_nonuniform_df$adj_p) & neu_nonuniform_df$adj_p < .05, "Flagged", "No")
)

neu_both_tbl <- tibble(
  Scale     = neu_scale,
  Effect    = "Combined DIF (a1 + d)",
  Item      = neu_both_df$Item,
  X2        = neu_both_df$X2,
  df        = neu_both_df$df,
  p         = neu_both_df$p,
  adj_p     = neu_both_df$adj_p,
  DIF_flag  = ifelse(!is.na(neu_both_df$adj_p) & neu_both_df$adj_p < .05, "Flagged", "No")
)

neu_anchor_items <- neu_both_tbl %>% filter(DIF_flag == "No") %>% pull(Item)
neu_flagged_items <- neu_both_tbl %>% filter(DIF_flag == "Flagged") %>% pull(Item)

if (length(neu_anchor_items) < 2) stop("Neuroticism: too few anchor items.")

neu_anchor_model <- multipleGroup(
  data       = neu_dat,
  model      = 1,
  group      = neu_grp,
  itemtype   = "2PL",
  invariance = c("free_means", "free_var", neu_anchor_items),
  SE         = FALSE,
  verbose    = FALSE
)

neu_anchor_pars <- coef(neu_anchor_model, IRTpars = TRUE, simplify = TRUE)
neu_group_pars_tbl <- bind_rows(
  {
    x <- as.data.frame(neu_anchor_pars[[levels(neu_grp)[1]]]$items)
    x$Item <- rownames(x); x$Group <- levels(neu_grp)[1]; x$Scale <- neu_scale; x
  },
  {
    x <- as.data.frame(neu_anchor_pars[[levels(neu_grp)[2]]]$items)
    x$Item <- rownames(x); x$Group <- levels(neu_grp)[2]; x$Scale <- neu_scale; x
  }
)

neu_summary_tbl <- neu_both_tbl %>%
  summarise(
    Scale = neu_scale,
    n_items_tested = n(),
    n_flagged = sum(DIF_flag == "Flagged", na.rm = TRUE),
    flagged_items = ifelse(n_flagged == 0, "", paste(Item[DIF_flag == "Flagged"], collapse = ", "))
  )

# ============================================================
# 4. LIE
# ============================================================

lie_scale <- "Lie"
lie_items <- epq_item_sets[[lie_scale]]

lie_dat_raw <- as.data.frame(epq_items[, lie_items, drop = FALSE])
lie_grp_raw <- demographics$gender

lie_keep <- complete.cases(lie_dat_raw, lie_grp_raw)
lie_dat  <- lie_dat_raw[lie_keep, , drop = FALSE]
lie_grp  <- droplevels(factor(lie_grp_raw[lie_keep]))
if (ref_group %in% levels(lie_grp)) lie_grp <- relevel(lie_grp, ref = ref_group)
lie_dat[] <- lapply(lie_dat, function(x) as.numeric(as.character(x)))

lie_item_ok <- vapply(names(lie_dat), function(nm) {
  x <- lie_dat[[nm]]
  binary_ok <- all(na.omit(x) %in% c(0, 1))
  var_by_group <- tapply(x, lie_grp, function(z) length(unique(na.omit(z))) > 1)
  binary_ok && all(var_by_group)
}, logical(1))

lie_dat <- lie_dat[, lie_item_ok, drop = FALSE]

cat("\n--- Lie ---\n")
cat("N =", nrow(lie_dat), "\n")
print(table(lie_grp))
cat("Items retained:", paste(names(lie_dat), collapse = ", "), "\n")

lie_base <- multipleGroup(
  data       = lie_dat,
  model      = 1,
  group      = lie_grp,
  itemtype   = "2PL",
  invariance = c("free_means", "free_var", colnames(lie_dat)),
  SE         = FALSE,
  verbose    = FALSE
)

lie_uniform <- DIF(
  MGmodel    = lie_base,
  which.par  = "d",
  items2test = colnames(lie_dat),
  scheme     = "drop",
  simplify   = TRUE,
  p.adjust   = "BH",
  verbose    = FALSE
)

lie_nonuniform <- DIF(
  MGmodel    = lie_base,
  which.par  = "a1",
  items2test = colnames(lie_dat),
  scheme     = "drop",
  simplify   = TRUE,
  p.adjust   = "BH",
  verbose    = FALSE
)

lie_both <- DIF(
  MGmodel    = lie_base,
  which.par  = c("a1", "d"),
  items2test = colnames(lie_dat),
  scheme     = "drop",
  simplify   = TRUE,
  p.adjust   = "BH",
  verbose    = FALSE
)

lie_uniform_df <- as.data.frame(lie_uniform); lie_uniform_df$Item <- rownames(lie_uniform_df)
lie_nonuniform_df <- as.data.frame(lie_nonuniform); lie_nonuniform_df$Item <- rownames(lie_nonuniform_df)
lie_both_df <- as.data.frame(lie_both); lie_both_df$Item <- rownames(lie_both_df)

lie_uniform_tbl <- tibble(
  Scale     = lie_scale,
  Effect    = "Uniform DIF (d)",
  Item      = lie_uniform_df$Item,
  X2        = lie_uniform_df$X2,
  df        = lie_uniform_df$df,
  p         = lie_uniform_df$p,
  adj_p     = lie_uniform_df$adj_p,
  DIF_flag  = ifelse(!is.na(lie_uniform_df$adj_p) & lie_uniform_df$adj_p < .05, "Flagged", "No")
)

lie_nonuniform_tbl <- tibble(
  Scale     = lie_scale,
  Effect    = "Nonuniform DIF (a1)",
  Item      = lie_nonuniform_df$Item,
  X2        = lie_nonuniform_df$X2,
  df        = lie_nonuniform_df$df,
  p         = lie_nonuniform_df$p,
  adj_p     = lie_nonuniform_df$adj_p,
  DIF_flag  = ifelse(!is.na(lie_nonuniform_df$adj_p) & lie_nonuniform_df$adj_p < .05, "Flagged", "No")
)

lie_both_tbl <- tibble(
  Scale     = lie_scale,
  Effect    = "Combined DIF (a1 + d)",
  Item      = lie_both_df$Item,
  X2        = lie_both_df$X2,
  df        = lie_both_df$df,
  p         = lie_both_df$p,
  adj_p     = lie_both_df$adj_p,
  DIF_flag  = ifelse(!is.na(lie_both_df$adj_p) & lie_both_df$adj_p < .05, "Flagged", "No")
)

lie_anchor_items <- lie_both_tbl %>% filter(DIF_flag == "No") %>% pull(Item)
lie_flagged_items <- lie_both_tbl %>% filter(DIF_flag == "Flagged") %>% pull(Item)

if (length(lie_anchor_items) < 2) stop("Lie: too few anchor items.")

lie_anchor_model <- multipleGroup(
  data       = lie_dat,
  model      = 1,
  group      = lie_grp,
  itemtype   = "2PL",
  invariance = c("free_means", "free_var", lie_anchor_items),
  SE         = FALSE,
  verbose    = FALSE
)

lie_anchor_pars <- coef(lie_anchor_model, IRTpars = TRUE, simplify = TRUE)
lie_group_pars_tbl <- bind_rows(
  {
    x <- as.data.frame(lie_anchor_pars[[levels(lie_grp)[1]]]$items)
    x$Item <- rownames(x); x$Group <- levels(lie_grp)[1]; x$Scale <- lie_scale; x
  },
  {
    x <- as.data.frame(lie_anchor_pars[[levels(lie_grp)[2]]]$items)
    x$Item <- rownames(x); x$Group <- levels(lie_grp)[2]; x$Scale <- lie_scale; x
  }
)

lie_summary_tbl <- lie_both_tbl %>%
  summarise(
    Scale = lie_scale,
    n_items_tested = n(),
    n_flagged = sum(DIF_flag == "Flagged", na.rm = TRUE),
    flagged_items = ifelse(n_flagged == 0, "", paste(Item[DIF_flag == "Flagged"], collapse = ", "))
  )

# ============================================================
# 5. Combine outputs
# ============================================================

epq_2pl_uniform_tbl <- bind_rows(
  psy_uniform_tbl, ext_uniform_tbl, neu_uniform_tbl, lie_uniform_tbl
)

epq_2pl_nonuniform_tbl <- bind_rows(
  psy_nonuniform_tbl, ext_nonuniform_tbl, neu_nonuniform_tbl, lie_nonuniform_tbl
)

epq_2pl_both_tbl <- bind_rows(
  psy_both_tbl, ext_both_tbl, neu_both_tbl, lie_both_tbl
)

epq_2pl_group_pars_tbl <- bind_rows(
  psy_group_pars_tbl, ext_group_pars_tbl, neu_group_pars_tbl, lie_group_pars_tbl
)

epq_2pl_summary_tbl <- bind_rows(
  psy_summary_tbl, ext_summary_tbl, neu_summary_tbl, lie_summary_tbl
)

# ============================================================
# 6. APA-friendly tables
# ============================================================

# Table A: Subscale-level DIF summary
dif_summary_apa <- epq_2pl_summary_tbl %>%
  mutate(
    `Subscale` = Scale,
    `Items tested` = n_items_tested,
    `Flagged items (combined DIF)` = flagged_items
  ) %>%
  select(`Subscale`, `Items tested`, n_flagged, `Flagged items (combined DIF)`) %>%
  rename(`No. of DIF items` = n_flagged)

# Table B: Item-level combined DIF results
dif_item_apa <- epq_2pl_both_tbl %>%
  transmute(
    Subscale = Scale,
    Item,
    `χ²` = fmt_num(X2, 2),
    df = df,
    p = fmt_p(p),
    `BH-adjusted p` = fmt_p(adj_p),
    `Flagged` = DIF_flag
  )

# Table C: Group-specific item parameters from partially anchored models
group_pars_apa <- epq_2pl_group_pars_tbl %>%
  transmute(
    Subscale = Scale,
    Group,
    Item,
    a = fmt_num(a, 2),
    b = fmt_num(b, 2)
  )

# Table D: Only flagged items with group-specific parameters
flagged_items_all <- epq_2pl_both_tbl %>%
  filter(DIF_flag == "Flagged") %>%
  select(Scale, Item)

flagged_group_pars_apa <- epq_2pl_group_pars_tbl %>%
  semi_join(flagged_items_all, by = c("Scale", "Item")) %>%
  transmute(
    Subscale = Scale,
    Group,
    Item,
    a = fmt_num(a, 2),
    b = fmt_num(b, 2)
  )

# ============================================================
# 7. Export CSVs
# ============================================================

write_csv(epq_2pl_uniform_tbl,       file.path(out_dir, "epq_2pl_uniform_dif.csv"))
write_csv(epq_2pl_nonuniform_tbl,    file.path(out_dir, "epq_2pl_nonuniform_dif.csv"))
write_csv(epq_2pl_both_tbl,          file.path(out_dir, "epq_2pl_combined_dif.csv"))
write_csv(epq_2pl_group_pars_tbl,    file.path(out_dir, "epq_2pl_group_specific_item_pars.csv"))
write_csv(epq_2pl_summary_tbl,       file.path(out_dir, "epq_2pl_summary.csv"))

write_csv(dif_summary_apa,           file.path(out_dir, "apa_dif_summary.csv"))
write_csv(dif_item_apa,              file.path(out_dir, "apa_dif_item_results.csv"))
write_csv(group_pars_apa,            file.path(out_dir, "apa_group_item_parameters.csv"))
write_csv(flagged_group_pars_apa,    file.path(out_dir, "apa_flagged_item_parameters.csv"))

# ============================================================
# 8. papaja tables
# Run these in your R Markdown / papaja document
# ============================================================



# quick check
print(dif_summary_apa)


# ------------------------------------------------------------
# 1. By subscale
# ------------------------------------------------------------

uniform_summary <- epq_2pl_uniform_tbl %>%
  group_by(Scale) %>%
  summarise(
    n_uniform = sum(DIF_flag == "Flagged", na.rm = TRUE),
    uniform_items = ifelse(
      n_uniform == 0,
      "",
      paste(Item[DIF_flag == "Flagged"], collapse = ", ")
    ),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

nonuniform_summary <- epq_2pl_nonuniform_tbl %>%
  group_by(Scale) %>%
  summarise(
    n_nonuniform = sum(DIF_flag == "Flagged", na.rm = TRUE),
    nonuniform_items = ifelse(
      n_nonuniform == 0,
      "",
      paste(Item[DIF_flag == "Flagged"], collapse = ", ")
    ),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

both_summary <- epq_2pl_both_tbl %>%
  group_by(Scale) %>%
  summarise(
    n_both = sum(DIF_flag == "Flagged", na.rm = TRUE),
    both_items = ifelse(
      n_both == 0,
      "",
      paste(Item[DIF_flag == "Flagged"], collapse = ", ")
    ),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

dif_summary_by_scale <- uniform_summary %>%
  full_join(nonuniform_summary, by = "Scale") %>%
  full_join(both_summary, by = "Scale") %>%
  arrange(Scale)

dif_summary_by_scale
# ------------------------------------------------------------
# APA-friendly version
# ------------------------------------------------------------

dif_summary_apa <- dif_summary_by_scale %>%
  transmute(
    Subscale = Scale,
    `Uniform DIF, n` = n_uniform,
    `Uniform DIF items` = uniform_items,
    `Nonuniform DIF, n` = n_nonuniform,
    `Nonuniform DIF items` = nonuniform_items,
    `Combined DIF, n` = n_both,
    `Combined DIF items` = both_items
  )

dif_summary_apa
# Optional export
write_csv(dif_summary_apa, file.path(out_dir, "dif_summary_apa.csv"))

```

```{r}
papaja::apa_table(
  dif_summary_apa,
  caption = "Summary of 2PL differential item functioning results across EPQ-RS-B subscales.",
  note = "DIF was evaluated using multiple-group 2PL models. Flagged items are based on the combined DIF test (a1 + d) with Benjamini-Hochberg adjusted p values."
)

```

```{r}
papaja::apa_table(
  dif_item_apa,
  caption = "Item-level combined DIF results for the EPQ-RS-B subscales.",
  note = "Combined DIF reflects simultaneous testing of discrimination (a1) and difficulty/intercept (d) parameters. BH = Benjamini-Hochberg."
)

```


```{r}
papaja::apa_table(
  flagged_group_pars_apa,
  caption = "Group-specific 2PL item parameters for flagged DIF items.",
  note = "Parameters were extracted from partially anchored multiple-group 2PL models in which non-flagged items were constrained equal across groups."
)

```





## Concurrent Validity With BFI-2

```{r concurrent-validity, include=FALSE}
cross_framework_cor <- psych::corr.test(
  bfi_domains,
  epq_scores,
  method = "pearson",
  adjust = "holm"
)

cross_r <- as.data.frame(round(cross_framework_cor$r, 3)) |>
  tibble::rownames_to_column("BFI-2 Domain")

cross_p <- as.data.frame(cross_framework_cor$p) |>
  tibble::rownames_to_column("BFI-2 Domain")
```

Table \@ref(tab:concurrent) presents the correlation matrix between EPQ-RS-B subscale scores and BFI-2-B domain scores. The concurrent validity pattern conformed to theoretical expectations.

```{r concurrent, results='asis'}
apa_table(
  cross_r,
  caption = "Pearson correlations between BFI-2-B domains and EPQ-RS-B subscales.",
  note = "All coefficients Holm-adjusted. Bold values indicate convergent validity pairs."
)
```

# Discussion

The present study provides the first psychometric evaluation of a Bangla adaptation of the EPQ-RS. The results largely replicate the cross-cultural pattern that has emerged across more than a dozen language adaptations over four decades: Extraversion and Neuroticism subscales demonstrated acceptable reliability, coherent factor structure, and adequate measurement precision, whereas Psychoticism was markedly weaker on all criteria.

The reliability estimates for EPQ-RS-B Extraversion and Neuroticism fell within the range reported in English-speaking samples ($\alpha$ = .78 to .88; @eysenck1985revised; @francis1992abbreviated), Japanese ($\alpha$ = .75 to .78; @hosokawa1993reliability), Greek ($\alpha$ = .73 to .78; @alexopoulos2004psychometric), and German ($\alpha$ = .81 to .85; @francis2006german) adaptations. The Lie scale performed in the range typical of non-English versions. Psychoticism reliability in the present sample was consistent with the range of .24 to .62 that characterizes the cross-cultural literature, falling below the conventional .70 threshold regardless of which estimator was employed. This finding is not attributable to translation quality alone; it is the modal outcome for this subscale across languages and continents.

The IRT analyses added precision to this picture. The 2PL models revealed that P items had, on average, lower discrimination parameters and more extreme difficulty values than items from other subscales, resulting in a test information function that peaked at unusual theta levels and provided limited measurement precision across the practically relevant range. By contrast, E and N items showed discrimination and information patterns consistent with adequate measurement. These IRT findings converge with the classical indices and with the Italian IRT analysis by @colledani2018irt, who found that even after IRT-guided item selection, P remained the weakest EPQ-RS scale.

## Invariance
The invariance results for the EPQ-RS-B follow a pattern that is well documented in the measurement invariance literature. Full configural and metric invariance were established with ease, indicating that males and females interpreted the items in terms of the same latent constructs and that the strength of item-factor relations did not differ across groups. This is consistent with prior findings on Eysenckian measures, where the basic factor structure of extraversion, neuroticism, psychoticism, and the lie scale has been repeatedly recovered across sex and cultural groups (Barrett et al., 1998; Eysenck & Barrett, 2013; Francis et al., 1992). McLarnon and Romero (2020) similarly reported that the configural structure of shortened EPQ forms was broadly replicable across 35 countries, though they cautioned that metric and scalar equivalence required alignment corrections before cross-group comparisons could be trusted.
The localized strain observed at M10, when latent means were first constrained to equality, warrants careful interpretation. This step effectively tests whether males and females occupy the same position on each latent dimension, and its failure is not a measurement artefact but rather a substantive finding: the sexes differ in their standing on one or more of the Eysenckian traits. Such differences are among the most robustly replicated results in personality psychology. Females consistently score higher on neuroticism, while males tend to score higher on psychoticism (Alexopoulos & Kalaitzidis, 2004; Aluja et al., 2003; Hosokawa & Ohyama, 1993). These mean-level differences are expected to produce misfit when latent means are forcibly equated, and this is precisely what the M10 results reflect.
The critical observation is that M13, the fully constrained model, met practical fit-change criteria. This pattern, in which intermediate mean-structure steps show strain but the terminal model is tenable, aligns with the interpretive logic articulated by Marsh et al. (2009), who argued that the most restrictive solution provides the primary evidential basis for concluding that cross-group comparisons operate within a defensible invariant framework. From this perspective, the EPQ-RS-B can be regarded as measuring the same constructs, with the same metric and scalar properties, across sexes in the present sample. This does not deny that latent mean differences exist; rather, it confirms that such differences, once incorporated into a model that also constrains all other relevant parameters, do not compromise the overall measurement framework.
It is worth noting that the recovery of fit from M10 to M13 is itself informative. The addition of residual variance and factor variance/covariance constraints in M13 evidently compensated for the strain introduced by mean constraints alone, suggesting that the misfit at M10 was partly attributable to unmodeled heterogeneity in variance parameters rather than to genuine intercept non-invariance. This interpretation is consistent with Chen's (2007) demonstration that fit-change indices are jointly sensitive to differences in means, variances, and covariances, making it hazardous to attribute misfit at any single step to a single parameter class in isolation.
The present findings should be situated alongside the well-known limitations of the psychoticism scale, which has historically exhibited low internal consistency (Eysenck & Eysenck, 1985; Alexopoulos & Kalaitzidis, 2004; Francis et al., 1992) and has been shown to contain items with gender differential item functioning (Colledani et al., 2018). Although the current invariance analysis did not isolate scale-specific sources of strain, the broader literature suggests that psychoticism items are the most likely candidates for partial non-invariance at the item level. Future studies would benefit from item-level invariance testing or alignment methods (McLarnon & Romero, 2020) to pinpoint whether the localized misfit at M10 is driven predominantly by the psychoticism or lie subscales. Despite these caveats, the overall conclusion is that the EPQ-RS-B demonstrates adequate sex measurement invariance under the 13-model framework, supporting the validity of cross-sex comparisons of Eysenckian personality dimensions in this sample.

##  Language Based Invariance
The language-invariance analyses yield a clear but qualified conclusion: the four-factor P, E, N, L structure of the EPQ-RS-B is configurally supported across groups, but the equality of factor loadings, thresholds, and higher-order parameters is not. Relative to the gender-invariance results reported earlier in which M13 met both decision rules for the EPQ-RS-B (ΔCFI = .002; ΔRMSEA = −.001), the language comparison produces a qualitatively different picture. The same instrument that behaved equivalently for men and women in this sample does not behave equivalently across the two language administrations. On its face, this pattern suggests that the constraint being tested by the language contrast is substantially more demanding than the within-language, cross-gender contrast, and that the observed item-level functioning carries language-specific variation in loadings and thresholds that cannot be fully absorbed by a common measurement model.
Three features of the result deserve emphasis rather than being smoothed over. First, the non-convergence of M5, M7, and M9 is not noise to be discarded; it signals that specific combinations of constraints, most notably those involving uniqueness equality together with intermediate mean-structure restrictions, are not jointly supported by these data. In categorical ESEM with binary items under theta parameterisation, uniqueness constraints have known identification subtleties (Bowen & Masa, 2015; Liu et al., 2017), and the failure to converge is consistent with the view that forcing equality across groups at these steps imposes more structure than the language-specific response patterns can bear. Second, the negative scaled Δχ² at M12 is not a substantive improvement in fit. It is a known behaviour of scaled-difference testing under WLSMV when the scaling factors are adjusted across nested models (Asparouhov & Muthén, 2010), and we flag it here so that subsequent readers do not over-interpret the apparent ΔCFI and ΔRMSEA gains at that step. Third, and most importantly, the ΔRMSEA-based rule indicated invariance at every converged step, while the ΔCFI-based rule did not. This divergence is by now a familiar story in the invariance literature, particularly with large samples and many items: RMSEA's parsimony correction tends to mask cross-group parameter differences that CFI detects (Chen, 2007; Khojasteh & Lo, 2015; Putnick & Bornstein, 2016). The most defensible reading of our data is the CFI-based one. Reporting only ΔRMSEA would give a misleadingly benign picture of the instrument's cross-language behaviour.
In substantive terms, the failure of metric invariance (M2) means that the factor loadings, that is, the calibrations by which items map onto their latent traits, are not equivalent across the two language groups. This is the most consequential single finding of the language-invariance analyses. When loadings differ across groups, the latent construct itself is not being indexed on a common metric, which in turn means that observed-score comparisons, effect sizes involving EPQ-RS-B scales, and correlation magnitudes with external criteria are not guaranteed to be comparable across languages (Meredith, 1993; Vandenberg & Lance, 2000). The failure of threshold invariance (M4) adds a second, compounding problem for observed-score comparisons: even if loadings were equal, unequal thresholds mean that the probability of endorsing a given item at a given level of the latent trait differs across language groups, producing systematic shifts in sum scores that are not attributable to true trait differences (Millsap, 2011). Given our earlier finding that the Psychoticism subscale is the weakest in internal consistency and IRT fit (a finding that replicates across 16+ cross-cultural EPQ-RS adaptations; see Introduction), it is plausible, although not directly tested here, that the Psychoticism and Lie items contribute disproportionately to the language non-invariance. Targeted partial-invariance or alignment analyses (Asparouhov & Muthén, 2014; Marsh et al., 2018) would be required to localise the problematic items.
The practical implication is that direct cross-language comparisons of EPQ-RS-B sum scores should be interpreted with caution. Within each language, the scales appear to measure a coherent four-factor structure; between languages, the measurement model is not strict enough to warrant treating sum-score differences, or even regression coefficients with external criteria, as unambiguously reflecting true trait differences. This is an ordinary consequence of adapting a binary, content-heterogeneous instrument across linguistic and cultural boundaries, and it is consistent with McLarnon and Romero's (2020) finding that cross-country equivalence of even shortened EPQ versions is supported only under partial-invariance frameworks or approximate-invariance methods such as alignment. Future work on the EPQ-RS-B should therefore extend beyond the exact-invariance taxonomy used here to the alignment method and item-level DIF, both of which are better suited to locating the specific items that drive non-invariance and to producing aligned scores that support latent-mean comparisons despite partial non-invariance.
Two caveats temper these conclusions. First, the taxonomy used here is the exact-invariance framework, which is known to be overly strict in many realistic cross-cultural applications (Marsh et al., 2018; van de Schoot et al., 2013). Approximate-invariance and alignment approaches often find that what appears to be a wholesale failure under exact invariance is in fact a localised failure involving a minority of items. Second, the non-convergence of three models means that the taxonomy, as applied, is incomplete. A full picture of language invariance for this instrument will require an alignment-based analysis in a follow-up study, and we plan to incorporate this as a supplementary analysis before submission.

## IRT
### Neuroticism, Extraversion, and the Lie Scale
The IRT analysis revealed a clear hierarchy of measurement quality across the four EPQ-RS-B subscales. Neuroticism performed best, with uniformly high item discriminations, well-distributed difficulty parameters, and a broad window of reliable measurement. This finding is consistent with prior IRT work on the EPQ-R Neuroticism scale. For example, Smits et al. (2021), using UK Biobank data from over 400,000 participants, reported that the 12 Neuroticism items showed generally strong discrimination, though they also noted that a reduced 7-item version was more psychometrically efficient because it eliminated items with redundant difficulty values. In the present Bangla sample, the full 12-item Neuroticism scale retained strong measurement properties, and the range of difficulty parameters was sufficiently broad to avoid the clustering problem identified by Smits et al. (2021). The marginal reliability of .83 and the empirical reliability of .80 place the Bangla Neuroticism scale comfortably within the range reported in cross-cultural adaptations, where alpha coefficients typically fall between .77 and .83 (Francis et al., 1992; Katz & Francis, 2000; Morfaki, 2021).

Extraversion and the Lie scale both achieved marginal reliabilities of .76, which are acceptable for research purposes though modest for individual-level assessment. These values are broadly comparable to the alpha coefficients reported in other non-Western adaptations. Francis et al. (2006) reported an Extraversion alpha of .85 in a German sample, and Katz and Francis (2000) reported values ranging from .78 to .87 across English-speaking countries. The somewhat lower reliability in the present sample may reflect cultural differences in the behavioral referents of Extraversion items, particularly those tapping sociability and gregariousness, which may carry different connotations in Bangladeshi collectivist cultural contexts (Hosokawa & Ohyama, 1993). IRT analysis further revealed that the Lie scale contained three weak items (EYP4, EYP16, EYP45) with low discrimination, and that EYP4 was essentially inert, failing to contribute any meaningful measurement information. These findings parallel those of Colledani et al. (2019), who identified similar weak items in the Italian EPQ-R and proposed shortened versions with improved psychometric efficiency.
### The Psychoticism Problem
The most consequential finding concerns the Psychoticism subscale. The marginal reliability of .44 and the negative empirical reliability confirm that the Bangla Psychoticism scale is not functioning as a coherent measure of a single latent trait. This result is not surprising when considered against the extensive literature documenting the poor psychometric performance of EPQ Psychoticism scales across cultures. Francis et al. (1992) reported alpha coefficients of .31 to .52 for the EPQ-RS Psychoticism scale across four English-speaking countries. Comparable or lower values have been reported in German (α = .42; Francis et al., 2006), Hindi (α = .24; Tiwari et al., 2009), Greek (α = .51; Morfaki, 2021), Japanese (α = .48; Hosokawa & Ohyama, 1993), and Hebrew (α = .31 to .41; Katz & Francis, 2000) adaptations. McLarnon and Romero (2020), using factor alignment across 35 countries from the Eysenck and Barrett (2013) archival dataset, concluded that the Psychoticism scale demonstrated weak reliability across many nations and recommended the use of latent variable approaches to partially mitigate measurement error concerns.

The IRT analysis in the present study goes further than classical reliability in diagnosing the source of the problem. The negative discrimination for EYP2 suggests that this item is operating in a direction opposite to the construct it is supposed to measure, possibly because of cultural differences in the interpretation of social conformity items. Two additional items (EYP6, EYP31) were essentially non-discriminating. The extreme difficulty values for several items (e.g., EYP6, b = 8.49; EYP28, b = 4.05) indicate that these items are endorsed only at implausibly extreme trait levels, rendering them uninformative for the vast majority of respondents. The TIC analysis confirmed that Psychoticism provides reliable measurement only for a narrow band of the population (θ = 1.65 to 2.87), approximately 1.5 to 3 standard deviations above the mean. For typical respondents, the scale generates more noise than signal.
These findings carry both theoretical and practical implications. Theoretically, the persistent failure of Psychoticism items across cultures calls into question whether the construct, as operationalized by Eysenck's items, captures a coherent dimension of individual differences or instead conflates heterogeneous content (e.g., nonconformity, impulsivity, callousness) that does not cohere into a unitary factor in many cultural contexts (Colledani et al., 2019; Eysenck & Barrett, 2013). Prior factor analytic work has repeatedly found that the P scale defines a multifaceted construct with poor unidimensionality (Howarth, 1986; Roger & Morris, 1991; Colledani et al., 2019). The IRT evidence from the present Bangla adaptation reinforces this conclusion by showing that the items not only fail to cohere internally but also fail to provide informative measurement at any commonly occurring trait level.
Practically, these results warrant caution in interpreting Psychoticism scores from the EPQ-RS-B at the individual level. Researchers wishing to assess constructs related to the Psychoticism domain in Bangladeshi samples may benefit from considering alternative instruments or, at a minimum, from interpreting Psychoticism scores only at the group level, where aggregation can attenuate measurement error. Future work on the EPQ-RS-B might also explore item replacement or the development of culturally appropriate Psychoticism indicators, building on the item-reduction strategies proposed by Colledani et al. (2019) and the shortened scale recommendations of McLarnon and Romero (2020).
The Wright map evidence reinforces and extends the precision concerns identified through the TIC and IIC analyses. Among the four EPQ-RS-B subscales, Extraversion showed the most balanced targeting, with item difficulties distributed across the person range in a manner that supports adequate measurement precision for most respondents. This finding is consistent with the broad effective theta coverage reported for Extraversion (-2.83 to 2.07) and its acceptable marginal reliability.
Neuroticism displayed generally sound targeting across its central range, but two high-difficulty outliers (EYP9 and EYP30) deserve scrutiny. These items sit at difficulty values exceeding 3, a region occupied by very few respondents. In a Bangladeshi sample, items such as "Are you an irritable person?" and "Would you call yourself tense or 'highly strung'?" may carry culturally specific connotations that inflate their effective difficulty. This does not necessarily indicate item malfunction, but it does mean these items contribute little to overall scale precision and are informationally redundant for most practical purposes. The remaining ten items, however, provide well-graduated coverage, which explains why Neuroticism maintains the strongest marginal reliability among the four subscales.
The Lie scale revealed an informative structural gap. The clustering of moderate-difficulty items between -1 and 0, combined with two extreme outliers above 3, leaves a measurement void in the 0 to 2.5 range. This gap means that the scale differentiates well among individuals with low social desirability tendencies, differentiates somewhat among those with very high tendencies, but performs poorly for the substantial middle-to-upper portion of the distribution. From a psychometric standpoint, this is the region where the Lie scale is most often called upon to function as a validity indicator, because it is precisely the moderately elevated Lie scorers who may be inflating their self-reports without reaching obviously implausible extremes. The inability to measure precisely in this range weakens the practical utility of the Lie scale as a response validity check in this population.
The most consequential finding concerns Psychoticism. The Wright map confirms what the TIC analysis suggested: the person-item misalignment is severe. Most item difficulties sit one to three standard deviations above the modal person location. This pattern is not unique to the present Bangladeshi sample. Cross-cultural studies of the EPQ-R have repeatedly documented that Psychoticism items tend to be rarely endorsed, producing extreme difficulty values and low internal consistency across diverse populations (Barrett & Eysenck, 1984; Eysenck et al., 1985; Francis et al., 2006). The present Wright map provides direct visual confirmation that, for the vast majority of Bangladeshi respondents, the Psychoticism items are effectively "off-target," functioning at a trait level that almost nobody in the sample occupies. The missing difficulty points for EYP2, EYP6, and EYP28 further suggest computational instability, likely driven by extremely low endorsement rates that prevent stable parameter estimation under the 2PL model.
This targeting failure carries direct implications for the comparative evaluation of the BFI-2-B and EPQ-RS-B frameworks. Any observed attenuation in Psychoticism's criterion correlations or predictive validity cannot be attributed solely to substantive trait irrelevance. It may instead reflect, at least in part, a measurement artifact: a scale that measures imprecisely across most of the score range will necessarily produce attenuated associations with external variables, regardless of how substantively relevant the underlying construct may be. The BFI-2-B, with its polytomous response format generating multiple thresholds per item, is structurally less vulnerable to this kind of targeting failure because even a four-item facet contributes four category boundaries rather than a single binary threshold.
These results reinforce the longstanding concern that the EPQ-R Psychoticism scale requires substantial revision for use in non-Western, non-English-speaking populations, and they suggest that caution is warranted when interpreting low Psychoticism scores as meaningful individual differences in this sample. More broadly, the Wright map evidence illustrates a limitation of the Giant Three framework that is not purely theoretical but rooted in the measurement properties of its operationalization: the Psychoticism construct, however important it may be conceptually, is not adequately captured by the EPQ-RS-B items in the present population.

## DIF

The Rasch-based DIF analysis revealed a differentiated pattern of gender measurement invariance across the four EPQ-RS subscales. The Lie scale was the most invariant: although the omnibus test was significant, no individual item survived correction, suggesting that the departure from invariance is diffuse and small in magnitude. The Psychoticism and Extraversion scales each contained a single item with significant DIF, indicating that these scales are largely gender-equivalent at the item level, with localised exceptions. The Neuroticism scale, by contrast, showed the most pervasive DIF, with five of twelve items exhibiting significant difficulty differences across gender.
The finding that EYP48 on Extraversion displayed the single largest DIF effect (delta-beta = -1.07, favouring male endorsement) converges with the earlier 2PL-based analysis, which also flagged this item via both Lord's chi-square and Raju's area methods. This convergence across two different IRT frameworks (one requiring separate group calibrations, the other using joint calibration) strengthens confidence that EYP48 functions differently across gender groups and is not merely an artifact of a particular estimation approach. Content inspection of EYP48 would be warranted to determine whether the item taps a behavioural domain with known gender-differential base rates (e.g., sensation seeking, social boldness) rather than the core Extraversion trait.
On the Psychoticism scale, the Rasch analysis flagged only one item (EYP43), in stark contrast to the earlier 2PL-based analysis that flagged 9 of 12 items. This discrepancy supports the methodological concern raised previously: the 2PL DIF results for Psychoticism reflected estimation failure (near-zero focal-group discriminations and extreme difficulty values) rather than genuine differential functioning. The Rasch model, by constraining discrimination to equality and using CML estimation, produced stable and interpretable parameters for all Psychoticism items, including those with low endorsement rates. The single flagged item (EYP43, delta-beta = 0.78) represents a credible and interpretable finding: this item was harder for males to endorse at equivalent trait levels. Future research should examine whether this reflects genuine content-based DIF or an interaction between the item's content domain (potentially more Agreeableness- than Conscientiousness-related, following Ruch et al., 2021) and known gender differences in Agreeableness.
The Neuroticism results merit careful interpretation. Five items displayed statistically significant DIF, but the direction of effects was mixed: two items (EYP9 and EYP42) were easier for males, while three (EYP13, EYP17, EYP38) were harder. When DIF effects operate in opposing directions within a scale, they can partially cancel at the total-score level, producing what Shealy and Stout (1993) termed "cancellation DIF." This means that the Neuroticism total score may be less biased than the item-level results suggest, because the male-advantaging and female-advantaging items offset one another. Nonetheless, five of twelve items is a substantial proportion, and this finding warrants scrutiny. Prior research on the EPQ-RS has generally found the Neuroticism scale to be less affected by gender DIF than Psychoticism (Colledani et al., 2018; Forrest et al., 2000), making the present results somewhat atypical. Whether this reflects cultural specificity of the Bangla adaptation, sample characteristics, or methodological sensitivity (the Rasch Wald test may be more powerful than the methods used in prior studies) cannot be determined from the present data alone.
The Lie scale results are reassuring. Despite the significant omnibus LR test, the absence of any individually flagged item after correction suggests that the scale's invariance violation is distributed across many items at trivially small magnitudes. This is a common pattern with omnibus tests: when sample sizes are adequate (here, N > 500), the LR test has sufficient power to detect aggregate departures that are too small to localise at the item level. From a practical standpoint, the Lie scale can be considered gender-equivalent.
Several methodological points deserve comment. First, the choice of the Rasch model for DIF rather than the 2PL was driven by the data, not by theoretical preference. The 2PL is the more general model and was used for the main IRT calibration on the full sample, where it performed adequately. However, when the task shifts to group-specific parameter recovery with only 12 binary items per group, the additional discrimination parameter becomes a liability rather than an asset, particularly for scales with low reliability and skewed item distributions. The Rasch model provided stable, interpretable estimates across all four scales and both gender groups, which the 2PL did not. This outcome illustrates a broader principle in psychometric practice: the most appropriate model depends not only on theoretical considerations but also on the inferential task and the data conditions under which it must operate (Embretson & Reise, 2000).
Second, the omnibus Andersen LR test was significant for all four scales, including those where item-level DIF was minimal or absent. This highlights the well-known sensitivity of omnibus invariance tests in adequately powered samples and cautions against interpreting omnibus significance as evidence that a scale is problematically biased. Item-level follow-up is essential for evaluating practical impact.
Third, the present analysis examined only uniform DIF (differences in item difficulty). The Rasch model does not accommodate non-uniform DIF (differences in item discrimination across groups), because discrimination is constrained to equality by definition. If non-uniform DIF is suspected on substantive grounds, complementary methods such as logistic regression (Swaminathan & Rogers, 1990) could be applied. However, given the estimation instability observed with the 2PL (which does allow discrimination to vary), the practical value of testing non-uniform DIF with these data is questionable.


The concurrent validity results confirmed the expected pattern of associations between PEN and Big Five domains. EPQ-RS-B Extraversion correlated positively with BFI-2-B Extraversion, EPQ-RS-B Neuroticism correlated positively with BFI-2-B Negative Emotionality, and EPQ-RS-B Psychoticism correlated negatively with BFI-2-B Agreeableness and Conscientiousness. These associations are consistent with the theoretical mapping outlined by @mccrae1985comparison and supported empirically by @colledani2018irt and @ruch2021long, who showed that P content has shifted across revisions toward a blend of low Agreeableness and low Conscientiousness.

The structural validity evaluation using ESEM with target rotation provided a more realistic representation of the EPQ-RS-B item structure than strict CFA. This finding parallels @aluja2003psychometric, who demonstrated that CFA simple-structure models for the Spanish EPQ-RS produced poor fit indices and that modification indices pointed to systematic cross-loadings and local dependencies. ESEM allowed small but meaningful cross-loadings to be estimated rather than forced to zero, resulting in more interpretable fit indices without sacrificing theoretical grounding.

Several limitations warrant mention. First, the sample was recruited through convenience sampling in Dhaka, limiting representativeness. Second, the fixed instrument order (BFI-2-B preceding EPQ-RS-B) may have introduced order effects. Third, no test-retest data were collected, precluding evaluation of temporal stability. Fourth, the absence of an external criterion measure beyond the BFI-2-B limits the scope of concurrent validity evidence. Fifth, the sample composition (predominantly Muslim, urban, and educated) may not generalize to rural or less-educated Bangladeshi populations.

In conclusion, the EPQ-RS-B provides an adequate Bangla-language measure of Extraversion and Neuroticism, with psychometric properties comparable to those observed in other non-English adaptations. The Psychoticism scale, however, cannot be recommended for use in its current form without substantial reservations. Researchers employing the EPQ-RS-B in Bangladeshi contexts should interpret P scores with caution and consider supplementing PEN assessment with broader-bandwidth instruments such as the BFI-2-B when measurement of the personality space encompassed by low Agreeableness and low Conscientiousness is required.

# Limitation
It should be noted that both the Andersen LR test and the item-level Wald test, as implemented within the dichotomous Rasch model, are tests of uniform DIF only. That is, they evaluate whether item difficulty parameters differ across groups under the assumption that the discrimination parameter is fixed at unity for all items and groups. Non-uniform DIF, which would manifest as group-by-ability interactions (i.e., crossing item characteristic curves), is not directly testable within the standard Rasch framework because the model constrains all discrimination parameters to be equal. Consequently, the present analysis cannot rule out the presence of non-uniform DIF. Researchers interested in detecting non-uniform DIF with these data would need to employ alternative approaches, such as logistic regression DIF methods (Swaminathan & Rogers, 1990) or interaction terms within two-parameter IRT models, which allow discrimination to vary across groups. This

\newpage

# References

::: {#refs custom-style="Bibliography"}
:::
